---
title: "U.S. DOE H2O Wave Hindcast"
subtitle: "Open-Source Data Resource Characterization Example"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
    embed-resources: true
  ipynb: default
  typst:
    papersize: us-letter
    margin:
      x: 0.75in
    toc: true
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

This examples shows how to use open source H2O hindcast resource characterization data from a single 
marine energy site and perform a high level wave energy resource analysis.

# Setup

```{python}
# --- Platform bootstrap --------------------------------------------------------
# Runs first, before numpy/pandas are imported, because installing mhkit on Kaggle
# can move those versions and an already-imported module would go stale. On a
# developer machine every branch below is a no-op.
import os
import shutil
import socket
import subprocess
import sys
from pathlib import Path

ON_KAGGLE = "KAGGLE_KERNEL_RUN_TYPE" in os.environ or Path("/kaggle/working").is_dir()

# Everything the document writes hangs off WORK_DIR. Locally `Path(".") / "figures"`
# is `figures`, so local behaviour is unchanged.
WORK_DIR = Path("/kaggle/working") if ON_KAGGLE else Path(".")

# auto  -- use cached pickles when present, fetch from S3 otherwise (the default)
# cache -- never touch S3; a missing key is a hard error
# live  -- ignore the seeded cache entirely, to prove the S3 path still works
DATA_MODE = os.environ.get("H2O_DATA_MODE", "auto")

# The companion Kaggle Dataset ships `cache/` (the warmed pickles) and
# `h2o_examples/` (the mapping module). Locate it by looking for the package.
KAGGLE_ASSETS = next(Path("/kaggle/input").glob("*/h2o_examples"), None) if ON_KAGGLE else None
KAGGLE_ASSETS = KAGGLE_ASSETS.parent if KAGGLE_ASSETS else None

if ON_KAGGLE:
    # Select the existing `dynamic` preset -- live Folium map, interactive tables.
    # setdefault, never assignment: `make render-pdf` sets =png and must win.
    os.environ.setdefault("H2O_RENDER_CONTEXT", "dynamic")
    # `import rex` comes from nlr-rex, pulled in by mhkit's wave extra. Do not
    # install PyPI `rex` -- unrelated project. No -U: Kaggle's numpy/pandas/scipy
    # already satisfy mhkit's floors and should be left alone.
    subprocess.run(
        [sys.executable, "-m", "pip", "install", "-q",
         "mhkit[wave]==1.1.*", "itables", "folium"],
        check=True,
    )
    if KAGGLE_ASSETS:
        sys.path.insert(0, str(KAGGLE_ASSETS))


def s3_reachable(host="wpto-pds-us-wave.s3.amazonaws.com", timeout=5):
    """True if the hindcast bucket is dialable. Probed once, then remembered."""
    if not hasattr(s3_reachable, "_result"):
        if DATA_MODE == "cache":
            s3_reachable._result = False
        else:
            try:
                socket.create_connection((host, 443), timeout=timeout).close()
                s3_reachable._result = True
            except OSError:
                s3_reachable._result = False
    return s3_reachable._result


def require_cached(paths, what):
    """Fail now, with a fix, rather than 40 cells from here.

    A missing pickle is only a problem when S3 is also out of reach. Silently
    dropping a site would produce a plausible-looking but wrong comparison -- the
    same class of error as reading the -9 fill value as a wave height.
    """
    missing = [p for p in paths if not Path(p).exists()]
    if not missing or s3_reachable():
        return
    names = "\n  ".join(sorted(str(p) for p in missing))
    raise RuntimeError(
        f"{what}: {len(missing)} cached file(s) missing and S3 is unreachable.\n"
        f"  {names}\n"
        "Fix either side: attach the companion Kaggle Dataset (it ships `cache/`), "
        "or enable Internet under Notebook settings so the data can be fetched."
    )
```

```{python}
import html
import inspect
import json

from urllib.parse import urlencode

import folium
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

from matplotlib.ticker import MultipleLocator
from sklearn.mixture import GaussianMixture

from mhkit.wave import contours, graphics, performance, resource
from rex import Resource

try:
    from h2o_examples.mapping import render_sites_map_outputs
except ImportError:
    # Kaggle without the companion dataset attached: the map section falls back to
    # a plain Folium map rather than taking the whole document down.
    render_sites_map_outputs = None


# Interactive DataTables everywhere except the Typst/PDF path, which cannot show
# them: the Makefile sets H2O_RENDER_CONTEXT=png for the PDF and =dynamic for the
# HTML and notebook. `show_table` renders an itables widget when interactive and a
# plain text table otherwise, so every output format shows *something* readable.
INTERACTIVE_TABLES = os.environ.get("H2O_RENDER_CONTEXT", "default") != "png"
if INTERACTIVE_TABLES:
    from itables import init_notebook_mode
    from itables import show as _show_interactive
    # itables defaults to connected=False, which embeds the DataTables JS rather
    # than fetching it from a CDN -- already the right behaviour everywhere, Kaggle
    # included. Do not pass connected=True: it makes every render wait on a CDN.
    init_notebook_mode(all_interactive=False)


def show_table(df, **kwargs):
    """Interactive itables view in HTML/notebook, static text in the PDF."""
    if INTERACTIVE_TABLES:
        _show_interactive(df, **kwargs)
    else:
        print(df.to_string())
```

## Figure Setup

```{python}

sns.set_theme()

# Save every figure to figures/<name>.png AND render inline.
# In a notebook plt.show()  draws inline;
# As a script (Agg) show() does not execute but the PNG is still written.
# `figures/*.png` is tracked with git-LFS (see .gitattributes) to keep the repo light.

FIG_DIR = WORK_DIR / "figures"
FIG_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)


def savefig(name):
    plt.gcf().savefig(FIG_DIR / f"{name}.png", dpi=150, bbox_inches="tight")
    plt.show()


def plot_wave_matrix(matrix, zlabel, figsize=(8, 8), fmt=".2f", buffer=1):
    """Heatmap of a sea-state matrix (Te on columns, Hm0 on the index).

    Empty bins are blanked and the axes are cropped to the populated bins plus a
    one-bin `buffer` on each side. Light minor gridlines fall on the bin edges.
    Adapted from the EMEC/PacWave proficiency-testing capture-matrix plot.
    """
    m = matrix.replace(0, np.nan)

    # Crop to populated bins + a one-bin buffer, dropping the empty low-Te block.
    rows = np.where(m.notna().any(axis=1))[0]
    cols = np.where(m.notna().any(axis=0))[0]
    r0, r1 = max(rows.min() - buffer, 0), min(rows.max() + buffer, m.shape[0] - 1)
    c0, c1 = max(cols.min() - buffer, 0), min(cols.max() + buffer, m.shape[1] - 1)
    m = m.iloc[r0 : r1 + 1, c0 : c1 + 1].sort_index(ascending=False)  # Hm0 up

    plt.figure(figsize=figsize)
    ax = sns.heatmap(
        m, annot=True, fmt=fmt, cmap="viridis", cbar_kws={"label": zlabel}
    )
    # Minor ticks sit on the bin edges (between the centered cells) -> grid there.
    ax.grid(False)
    ax.minorticks_on()
    ax.xaxis.set_minor_locator(MultipleLocator(1.0))
    ax.yaxis.set_minor_locator(MultipleLocator(1.0))
    ax.grid(which="minor", color="#dddddd", linewidth=0.6)
    ax.set_xlabel("Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]")
    ax.set_ylabel("Significant Wave Height, $H_{m0}$ [m]")
    plt.tight_layout()
    return ax
```

# Wave Energy Test Sites

```{python}
def centerpoint(corners):
    return {
        "lat": float(np.mean([corner["lat"] for corner in corners.values()])),
        "lng": float(np.mean([corner["lng"] for corner in corners.values()])),
    }


# Sites keyed by name -> {"lat": latitude, "lng": longitude, "label": short label}.
# PacWave sites also retain their original corners and calculate lat/lng from them.
# Run one now; loop later for multiple sites.
SITES = {
    # https://oregonstate.app.box.com/s/w9akpvhpev03mv4sqotl0vzm2dgk5xdq
    # PACWAVE SOUTH WAVE ENERGY
    # TEST SITE Testing Wave Energy for the Future
    # PacWave is an open ocean, wave energy testing facility at Oregon State University. It consists
    # of two sites, each located within several miles of the deep water commercial port of Newport,
    # Oregon. PacWave South is an in-development, state-of-the-art, pre-permitted, accredited,
    # grid- connected wave energy test facility; developed in partnership with the US Department of
    # Energy, the State of Oregon and local stakeholders. Construction started in 2021 and will be
    # completed in 2024, with testing starting in 2025.
    # SITE SPECIFICATIONS
    # • Number of berths: 4
    # • Location of Test Site: 6 nautical miles off the coast of Newport Oregon
    # • Depth of site: 65-78 meters MLLW
    # • Site coordinates:
    # NW: 44º 35’ 00.00”N 124º 14’ 30.00”W
    # NE: 44º 35’ 02.75”N 124º 13’ 06.17”W
    # SE: 44º 33’ 02.75”N 124º 12’ 58.51”W
    # SW: 44º 33’ 00.00”N 124º 14’ 22.41”W
    # • Nature of seabed: Soft, sandy bottom
    # • Wave data facilities: Waverider buoys, Spotter buoys, or similar
    # • Mean annual wave power density: 40 kW/m, varies with year and location
    # • Wave periods: 5-17s
    # • Prevailing wave direction: WNW
    # • Sea states: The majority of sea states are within the range of:
    # 1m < Hm0 < 3.5m and 7s < Te < 11s,
    # including extreme sea states caused by severe storms where Hm0 exceeded 7.5m.
    # • Environmental site characterization data
    # • Meteorological data
    # • Technical capacity: data acquisition, rated export capacity of berths:
    # 20MW Connection Voltage: 12.47kV to CLPUD, berths configurable up to 30kV
    # • Site access nearest port: Newport and Toledo portofnewport.com portoftoledo.org
    # • Support Facilities: Berthing & working areas, office facilities, boatyard
    # • Grid Connection: Metered at point of connection to the Central Lincoln People’s Utility Distric
    "US_Oregon_PacWave_South": {
        "label": "PacWave South",
        "color": "steelblue",
        "corners": {
            # NW: 44º 35’ 00.00”N 124º 14’ 30.00”W
            # https://www.google.com/maps/place/44%C2%B035'00.0%22N+124%C2%B014'30.0%22W/@44.5833333,-124.2416667,1231m/data=!3m2!1e3!4b1!4m4!3m3!8m2!3d44.5833333!4d-124.2416667?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
            "Northwest Corner": {"lat": 44.583333, "lng": -124.241667},
            # NE: 44º 35’ 02.75”N 124º 13’ 06.17”W
            # https://www.google.com/maps/place/44%C2%B035'02.8%22N+124%C2%B013'06.2%22W/@44.5840972,-124.2183806,1231m/data=!3m2!1e3!4b1!4m4!3m3!8m2!3d44.5840972!4d-124.2183806?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
            # Northeast Corner: (44.584097, -124.218381)
            "Northeast Corner": {"lat": 44.584097, "lng": -124.218381},
            # SW: 44º 33’ 00.00”N 124º 14’ 22.41”W
            # https://www.google.com/maps/place/44%C2%B033'00.0%22N+124%C2%B014'22.4%22W/@44.5507677,-124.2188277,1232m/data=!3m1!1e3!4m4!3m3!8m2!3d44.55!4d-124.2395583?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
            "Southwest Corner": {"lat": 44.550764, "lng": -124.216253},
            # SE: 44º 33’ 02.75”N 124º 12’ 58.51”W
            # https://www.google.com/maps/place/44%C2%B033'02.8%22N+124%C2%B012'58.5%22W/@44.5507677,-124.2188277,1232m/data=!3m2!1e3!4b1!4m4!3m3!8m2!3d44.5507639!4d-124.2162528?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
            "Southeast Corner": {"lat": 44.550000, "lng": -124.239558},
        },
    },
    # PACWAVE NORTH WAVE ENERGY
    # TEST SITE
    # SITE SPECIFICATIONS
    # Testing Wave Energy for the Future
    # PacWave is an open ocean wave energy testing facility at Oregon State University. It consists
    # of two sites, each located within several miles of the deep-water commercial port of Newport,
    # Oregon. PacWave North is an established autonomous test site for small-scale, prototype, and
    # maritime market technologies. PacWave North offers a site in state waters with streamlined
    # permitting; expected time to permit is under one year. The site is shallower than PacWave
    # South and closer to port. PacWave North is a persistently monitored site (wave, metocean
    # measurements, and habitat surveys).
    # • Flexible number of berths
    # • Located 2 nautical miles off the coastline
    # • Depth is 45-55 meters MLLW
    # • Site Coordinates:
    # NW: 44º 41’ 52.08”N 124º 08’ 46.32”W
    # NE: 44º 41’ 54.96”N 124º 07’ 22.44”W
    # SE: 44º 40’ 54.84”N 124º 07’ 18.48”W
    # SW: 44º 40’ 52.32”N 124º 08’ 42.72”W
    # • Seabed has a soft, sandy bottom
    # • Mean annual wave power density is 40kW/m,
    # variable with year/location
    # • Wave data facilities include Waverider
    # or similar buoys
    # • Wave periods are 5-17s
    # • Prevailing Wave Direction is WNW
    # • The majority of sea states are within the
    # range of:
    # 1m < H m0 < 3.5m and 7s < T e < 11s @ wvw
    # including extreme sea states caused by
    # severe storms where H m0 exceeded 7.5m
    # NW: 44º 41’ 52.08”N 124º 08’ 46.32”W
    # https://www.google.com/maps/place/44%C2%B041'52.1%22N+124%C2%B008'46.3%22W/@44.6978038,-124.1487749,1229m/data=!3m2!1e3!4b1!4m4!3m3!8m2!3d44.6978!4d-124.1462?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
    "US_Oregon_PacWave_North": {
        "label": "PacWave North",
        "color": "#d62728",
        "corners": {
            # NW: 44º 41’ 52.08”N 124º 08’ 46.32”W
            # https://www.google.com/maps/place/44%C2%B041'52.1%22N+124%C2%B008'46.3%22W/@44.6978038,-124.1487749,1229m/data=!3m2!1e3!4b1!4m4!3m3!8m2!3d44.6978!4d-124.1462?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
            # Northwest Corner: (44.697800, -124.146200)
            "Northwest Corner": {"lat": 44.697800, "lng": -124.146200},
            # NE: 44º 41’ 54.96”N 124º 07’ 22.44”W
            # https://www.google.com/maps/place/44%C2%B041'55.0%22N+124%C2%B007'22.4%22W/@44.6978038,-124.1487749,1229m/data=!3m1!1e3!4m4!3m3!8m2!3d44.6986!4d-124.1229?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
            # Northeast Corner: (44.698600, -124.122900)
            "Northeast Corner": {"lat": 44.698600, "lng": -124.122900},
            # SE: 44º 40’ 54.84”N 124º 07’ 18.48”W
            # https://www.google.com/maps/search/44%C2%BA+40%E2%80%99+54.84%E2%80%9DN+124%C2%BA+07%E2%80%99+18.48%E2%80%9DW/@44.6986038,-124.1254749,1229m/data=!3m1!1e3?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
            # Southeast Corner: (44.681900, -124.121800)
            "Southeast Corner": {"lat": 44.681900, "lng": -124.121800},
            # SW: 44º 40’ 52.32”N 124º 08’ 42.72”W
            # https://www.google.com/maps/place/44%C2%B040'52.3%22N+124%C2%B008'42.7%22W/@44.6812038,-124.1477749,1229m/data=!3m2!1e3!4b1!4m4!3m3!8m2!3d44.6812!4d-124.1452?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
            # Southwest Corner: (44.681200, -124.145200)
            "Southwest Corner": {"lat": 44.681200, "lng": -124.145200},
        },
    },
    # Azura Deployment Site: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Azura_(wave_power_device)
    "US_Hawaii_Oahu_WETS": {
        "lat": 21.46488,
        "lng": -157.751524,
        "label": "WETS",
    },
    # HERO WEC: https://modaq.nlr.gov/hero-wec-dashboard/?tab=location
    "US_North_Carolina_Jenettes_Pier": {
        "lat": 35.91036,
        "lng": -75.59239,
        "label": "Jennette's Pier",
    },
    # https://tethys-engineering.pnnl.gov/sites/default/files/publications/Coe-et-al-2023.pdf
    # NOTE: the key says Massachusetts but these coordinates are ~60 km off the
    # North Carolina coast, so this site sits in the Atlantic domain either way.
    "US_Massachusetts_WoodsHole_PioneerWec": {
        "lat": 35.943117,
        "lng": -74.88035,
        "label": "Pioneer WEC",
    },
    # CalWave Deployment: https://mhkdr.openei.org/files/322/CalWave_IOM_Revised.pdf
    "US_California_Scripps_Pier": {
        "lat": 32.867633,
        "lng": -117.263167,
        "label": "Scripps Pier",
    },
    # Best Guess
    # https://www.google.com/maps/@35.163837,-120.7279724,5653m/data=!3m1!1e3?entry=ttu&g_ep=EgoyMDI2MDcxMi4wIKXMDSoASAFQAw%3D%3D
    "US_California_San_Luis_Obispo": {
        "lat": 35.1672960606959,
        "lng": -120.740349224586,
        "label": "San Luis Obispo",
    },
    # Humboldt Bay, CA. Coordinates taken from the DOE-WPTO US Wave dataset viewer,
    # which reports this as location ID 596791: water depth 48 m, 5344.78 m to shore.
    # `nearest_gid` below independently resolves the same node -- gid 596791, depth
    # 48.06 m, 5344.78 m to shore -- which is a useful end-to-end check of the site
    # lookup against an authority outside this document. (The viewer lists its time
    # zone as 0; the file itself says -8, with jurisdiction "California".)
    "US_California_Humboldt_Bay": {
        "lat": 40.8398,
        "lng": -124.25,
        "label": "Humboldt Bay",
    },
    # The two sites below carry no test facility; they are open-water points chosen
    # to exercise the Alaska and Gulf of Mexico / Puerto Rico domains, which no
    # site above reaches.
    "US_Alaska_Kodiak": {
        "lat": 57.0,
        "lng": -152.5,
        "label": "Kodiak (open water)",
    },
    "US_PuertoRico_North_Shore": {
        "lat": 18.60,
        "lng": -66.10,
        "label": "N. of San Juan (open water)",
    },
}

# Which hindcast domain each site falls in. Stated explicitly rather than inferred:
# the domains overlap at their edges, and the file layout differs per region
# (see REGIONS under "Downloading Data").
SITE_REGIONS = {
    "US_Oregon_PacWave_South": "West_Coast",
    "US_Oregon_PacWave_North": "West_Coast",
    "US_California_San_Luis_Obispo": "West_Coast",
    "US_California_Scripps_Pier": "West_Coast",
    "US_California_Humboldt_Bay": "West_Coast",
    "US_Hawaii_Oahu_WETS": "Hawaii",
    "US_North_Carolina_Jenettes_Pier": "Atlantic",
    "US_Massachusetts_WoodsHole_PioneerWec": "Atlantic",
    "US_Alaska_Kodiak": "Alaska",
    "US_PuertoRico_North_Shore": "Gulf_of_Mexico_and_Puerto_Rico",
}

for site in SITES.values():
    if "corners" in site:
        site.update(centerpoint(site["corners"]))


def site_lat_lng(site):
    return (site["lat"], site["lng"])


def make_sites_map(sites):
    center = [
        np.mean([site["lat"] for site in sites.values()]),
        np.mean([site["lng"] for site in sites.values()]),
    ]
    site_map = folium.Map(location=center, zoom_start=4)

    for site in sites.values():
        if "corners" in site:
            add_site_bounds(site_map, site)

    for site in sites.values():
        folium.Marker(
            location=[site["lat"], site["lng"]],
            tooltip=site["label"],
        ).add_to(site_map)
        add_site_label(site_map, site)
    return site_map


def add_site_label(site_map, site):
    label = html.escape(site["label"])
    folium.Marker(
        location=[site["lat"], site["lng"]],
        icon=folium.DivIcon(
            icon_size=(150, 24),
            icon_anchor=(-8, 18),
            class_name="site-label",
            html=f"""
            <div style="
                color: #111827;
                font-size: 12px;
                font-weight: 600;
                line-height: 1.2;
                white-space: nowrap;
                text-shadow: -1px -1px 0 white, 1px -1px 0 white,
                             -1px 1px 0 white, 1px 1px 0 white;
                pointer-events: none;
            ">{label}</div>
            """,
        ),
    ).add_to(site_map)


def add_site_bounds(site_map, site):
    color = site["color"]
    bounds = [
        [corner["lat"], corner["lng"]] for corner in ordered_corners(site["corners"])
    ]
    folium.Polygon(
        locations=bounds,
        color=color,
        weight=2,
        fill=True,
        fill_color=color,
        fill_opacity=0.18,
        tooltip=folium.Tooltip(site["label"], permanent=False),
    ).add_to(site_map)


def ordered_corners(corners):
    ordered = sorted(corners.values(), key=lambda corner: corner["lat"], reverse=True)
    north = sorted(ordered[:2], key=lambda corner: corner["lng"])
    south = sorted(ordered[2:], key=lambda corner: corner["lng"], reverse=True)
    return [*north, *south]


def render_sites_map(sites, output_dir, context=None):
    if render_sites_map_outputs is not None:
        render_sites_map_outputs(
            sites,
            make_sites_map,
            output_dir=output_dir,
            stem="sites_map",
            context=context,
        )
    return make_sites_map(sites)


```

### Test Sites Map

```{python}
sites_map = render_sites_map(SITES, FIG_DIR)
```

### Test Site Coordinates

Every point plotted above, as a lat/lon pair. For the two PacWave sites this is the
centerpoint computed from the published corners, not a corner itself.

```{python}
# NREL Marine Energy Atlas deep links. The viewer's URL carries no per-point
# parameter -- clicking a marker only rewrites the `b` viewport bounds -- so the
# best available link is a bounding box drawn tightly around the site, which opens
# the map zoomed onto it. `vL` selects the displayed layer and is copied from the
# viewer; change it to link against a different layer.
ATLAS_URL = "https://maps.nrel.gov/marine-energy-atlas/data-viewer/data-library/layers"
ATLAS_LAYER = "68c955d8b12e9eb9d3c3fc55"
# Half-extents of the framing box, in degrees. ~2.2:1 to match the viewer's aspect.
ATLAS_HALF_LAT, ATLAS_HALF_LNG = 0.045, 0.10


def atlas_url(site, layer=ATLAS_LAYER):
    bounds = [
        [round(site["lng"] - ATLAS_HALF_LNG, 6), round(site["lat"] - ATLAS_HALF_LAT, 6)],
        [round(site["lng"] + ATLAS_HALF_LNG, 6), round(site["lat"] + ATLAS_HALF_LAT, 6)],
    ]
    query = urlencode({"vL": layer, "b": json.dumps(bounds, separators=(",", ":"))})
    return f"{ATLAS_URL}?{query}"


site_coordinates = pd.DataFrame(
    [
        {
            "Site": name,
            "Label": site["label"],
            "Region": SITE_REGIONS[name],
            "Latitude": site["lat"],
            "Longitude": site["lng"],
            # The static/PDF path prints the frame as text, where an anchor tag
            # would be noise; show the bare URL there instead.
            "Atlas": (
                f'<a href="{atlas_url(site)}" target="_blank">map</a>'
                if INTERACTIVE_TABLES
                else atlas_url(site)
            ),
        }
        for name, site in SITES.items()
    ]
)
show_table(site_coordinates)
```

Copy-paste form -- site name on one line, `latitude, longitude` on the next:

```{python}
for name, site in SITES.items():
    print(name)
    print(f"{site['lat']}, {site['lng']}")
```

## Downloading Data

We read the **3-hour bulk-parameter hindcast** straight from its source: the published
`.h5` files in the public `wpto-pds-us-wave` bucket on AWS S3. One request per site feeds
every deliverable below, `mean_wave_direction` included.

Three things about this dataset are worth stating plainly, because each one is a trap and
none of them is documented where you would look for it.

**Read S3, not HSDS.** The same data is exposed through HSDS at `/nrel/US_wave/...`, and
that mirror is partly broken: in any file whose `time_index` is stored with
`chunks=(1984,)`, reading the time index — even a single element — hangs for roughly 60
seconds and then fails with `OSError: Error retrieving data: None`. That afflicts every
West Coast file from 2011 onward and the whole CNMI/Guam domain. The wave data in those
files is readable; only the time axis is not, which is enough to make them useless. The S3
originals are intact. S3 also needs no API key and imposes no request limits, at the cost
of some speed, which the on-disk cache below absorbs.

**The latest version is not the same version everywhere.** `v1.0.1` extends the record to
2020 but exists only for West Coast, Atlantic, Alaska, and Gulf of Mexico / Puerto Rico.
Hawaii's newest release is still `v1.0.0`. There is no unversioned path that means
"latest", and file naming is not uniform either — the Gulf files are `GOM_PR_2010.h5`,
not `Gulf_of_Mexico_and_Puerto_Rico_wave_2010.h5`. `REGIONS` below therefore spells out
one explicit path per domain instead of deriving it from a template.

**Dataset names drift between domains.** The Gulf domain calls omnidirectional wave power
`omnidirectional_wave_power`, with no hyphen, where every other domain writes
`omni-directional_wave_power`; its water depth is `depth` rather than `water_depth`, and
its maximum-energy direction is `direction_of_maximum_directionally_resolved_wave_power`
rather than `maximum_energy_direction`. Ask for the wrong spelling and you get a
`KeyError`, not a fallback, so each region carries an alias map. The names can drift
between *years* of one domain too — Hawaii writes `water_depth` in 2010 but `depth` in
2020 — so treat the alias map as pinned to `YEAR`, not as a property of the domain.

```{python}
SELECTED_SITE = "US_Oregon_PacWave_South"
YEAR = 2010
lat_lon = site_lat_lng(SITES[SELECTED_SITE])
```

`YEAR = 2010` is deliberate, and it is the newest year that works everywhere. 2010 is the
last year present in every domain and every version; `v1.0.0` and `v1.0.1` are byte-for-byte
identical for it, so the version choice cannot skew a cross-region comparison. It is also
the last year whose files carry the `coordinates` dataset that the site lookup below
depends on — 2011+ files drop it — and the last year HSDS can read at all. A single year
is enough to exercise the pipeline; it is *not* enough for a formal extreme-value
estimate, a caveat repeated where it matters in Section 4.

```{python}
# Source data: the published .h5 files on AWS S3. Anonymous access, no API key.
S3_BUCKET = "s3://wpto-pds-us-wave"

# One entry per domain: the latest published version's path, plus any dataset names
# that domain spells differently from the canonical ones used in this document.
REGIONS = {
    "West_Coast": {
        "path": "v1.0.1/West_Coast/West_Coast_wave_{year}.h5",
        "aliases": {},
    },
    "Atlantic": {
        "path": "v1.0.1/Atlantic/Atlantic_wave_{year}.h5",
        "aliases": {},
    },
    "Alaska": {
        "path": "v1.0.1/Alaska/Alaska_wave_{year}.h5",
        "aliases": {},
    },
    "Hawaii": {
        # No v1.0.1 exists for Hawaii; v1.0.0 is the latest release.
        "path": "v1.0.0/Hawaii/Hawaii_wave_{year}.h5",
        "aliases": {},
    },
    "Gulf_of_Mexico_and_Puerto_Rico": {
        "path": "v1.0.1/Gulf_of_Mexico_and_Puerto_Rico/GOM_PR_{year}.h5",
        "aliases": {
            "omni-directional_wave_power": "omnidirectional_wave_power",
            "water_depth": "depth",
            "maximum_energy_direction": (
                "direction_of_maximum_directionally_resolved_wave_power"
            ),
        },
    },
    # CNMI_and_Guam is omitted: its HSDS copy is unreadable (see above) and no site
    # in this document falls in that domain. Reaching it via S3 would work.
}

PARAMS = [
    "significant_wave_height",
    "energy_period",
    "omni-directional_wave_power",
    "mean_wave_direction",
    "peak_period",
]

COLUMN_NAMES = {
    "significant_wave_height": "Hm0",
    "energy_period": "Te",
    "omni-directional_wave_power": "J",
    "mean_wave_direction": "Dir",
    "peak_period": "Tp",
}

# Sentinel written into the data for timesteps the model did not produce.
FILL_VALUE = -9.0

CACHE_DIR = WORK_DIR / (".cache" if DATA_MODE != "live" else ".cache-live")
CACHE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

# Seed from the companion Kaggle Dataset. Copy rather than read through it:
# `load_site` and `contour` write their results back, and /kaggle/input is
# read-only, so every miss would raise PermissionError. 6.7 MB copies in ~1 s and
# leaves a plain writable directory the existing cache logic uses untouched.
if DATA_MODE != "live" and KAGGLE_ASSETS and (KAGGLE_ASSETS / "cache").is_dir():
    shutil.copytree(KAGGLE_ASSETS / "cache", CACHE_DIR, dirs_exist_ok=True)


def cached(key, compute):
    """Memoise one small result to `.cache/<key>.pkl`.

    `load_site` covers the bulk sea-state records, but a few cells read the .h5
    files directly for things the masked cache cannot answer -- raw fill values,
    file metadata. Those reads are small in result and expensive in wall clock
    (~45 s for one year of one variable), so they are memoised the same way.
    """
    path = CACHE_DIR / f"{key}.pkl"
    if path.exists():
        return pd.read_pickle(path)
    value = compute()
    pd.to_pickle(value, path)
    return value
```

```{python}
def region_path(region, year):
    """Full S3 URI of the latest published file for a domain and year."""
    return f"{S3_BUCKET}/{REGIONS[region]['path'].format(year=year)}"


def nearest_gid(handle, lat_lon):
    """Index of the grid node closest to lat_lon, and that node's coordinates.

    Uses the `coordinates` dataset -- a plain (n, 2) float array, ~5.6 MB, one read.
    rex's own `lat_lon_gid` is the obvious alternative and the wrong one here: it
    pulls the 700k-row compound `meta` table and builds a scipy KD-tree over it,
    which costs minutes to locate a handful of points and is heavy enough to be
    refused outright over HSDS. A KD-tree pays off when querying many points against
    one grid; for a few sites, one argmin is faster than building the tree.
    """
    coords = handle["coordinates"]
    squared_distance = (coords[:, 0] - lat_lon[0]) ** 2 + (coords[:, 1] - lat_lon[1]) ** 2
    gid = int(np.argmin(squared_distance))
    return gid, (float(coords[gid, 0]), float(coords[gid, 1]))


def load_site(name, year=YEAR, params=PARAMS):
    """Hm0/Te/J/Dir for one site, from the latest published file for its domain.

    Returned as float64. The h5 datasets are float32, and that is not a harmless
    detail: five of mhkit's ten contour methods compute an internal default from the
    data (`max_x1 = x1.max() * 2`) and then reject it with
    `TypeError: max_x1 must be of type float`, because `np.float32` is not a Python
    float — `np.float64` is, which is why float64 input passes and float32 does not.
    The documented `max_x1`/`max_x2` kwargs cannot work around it: `environmental_contours`
    validates them and never forwards them to `_nonparametric_copula_parameters`.
    Casting once, here, keeps every method reachable.
    """
    cache_file = CACHE_DIR / f"{name}_{year}.pkl"
    if cache_file.exists():
        return pd.read_pickle(cache_file).astype("float64")

    region = SITE_REGIONS[name]
    aliases = REGIONS[region]["aliases"]
    lat_lon = site_lat_lng(SITES[name])

    with Resource(region_path(region, year)) as handle:
        gid, node = nearest_gid(handle, lat_lon)
        columns = {
            COLUMN_NAMES[p]: handle[aliases.get(p, p), :, gid] for p in params
        }
        df = pd.DataFrame(columns, index=handle.time_index).astype("float64")
        # Only the Gulf domain ships this; it lists timesteps the model skipped.
        missing = (
            {t.decode()[:19] for t in handle.h5["missing_time_index"][...]}
            if "missing_time_index" in handle.h5
            else set()
        )

    if missing:
        df = df[~df.index.strftime("%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S").isin(missing)]
    # Belt and braces: drop any fill the missing-time list did not account for.
    df = df[(df > FILL_VALUE + 0.1).all(axis=1)]

    df["J"] = df["J"] / 1000.0  # W/m -> kW/m
    df.attrs.update(region=region, gid=gid, node=node, year=year)
    df.to_pickle(cache_file)
    return df


data = load_site(SELECTED_SITE)
hindcast_region = data.attrs["region"]
```

### Gaps are filled with `-9`, not `NaN`

The Gulf of Mexico / Puerto Rico files carry a `missing_time_index` dataset naming the
timesteps the model never produced. At those timesteps every variable is written as
`-9.0`. It is a sentinel, not a measurement, and nothing in the file's attributes says so:
there is no `_FillValue`, no `NaN`, no mask. Load the file naively and pandas will hand
you a wave height of minus nine metres with a straight face.

For 2010 this affects **7 of 2920 timesteps** — 0.2% of the year — and that is exactly what
makes it dangerous. At the site used here it drags the annual mean $H_{m0}$ from 1.690 m
down to 1.664 m, a 1.5% error that looks like nothing and would never draw attention on its
own. The damage is downstream. A $-9$ m sea state lands far outside any real distribution,
so it stretches joint-probability bins, drags exceedance curves, and pulls hardest on
precisely the tails that the extreme-value contours in Section 4 are fitted to. A handful
of impossible points can move a 100-year contour more than a thousand ordinary ones.

`load_site` therefore drops the timesteps `missing_time_index` names, then filters any
remaining `-9` as a backstop — verified below to agree exactly. Only the Gulf domain has
gaps; the other four have no `missing_time_index` dataset and no fills at all. The general
lesson travels further than this dataset: **when a gridded product encodes "no data" as an
in-range number, an unmasked read fails silently rather than loudly.** Check for a fill
sentinel before trusting any summary statistic.

```{python}
# Confirm the two gap definitions agree, and that nothing impossible survives.
# The masked series comes from the cache; only the *raw* series has to be re-read,
# and its node index is already known, so the coordinate lookup is not repeated.
gulf_clean = load_site("US_PuertoRico_North_Shore")
gulf_gid = gulf_clean.attrs["gid"]

def read_gulf_raw():
    """Unmasked Hm0 and the file's own missing-timestep count, straight from S3."""
    with Resource(region_path("Gulf_of_Mexico_and_Puerto_Rico", YEAR)) as handle:
        return (
            handle["significant_wave_height", :, gulf_gid],
            int(handle.h5["missing_time_index"].shape[0]),
        )


gulf_raw, n_missing = cached(f"gulf_raw_{gulf_gid}_{YEAR}", read_gulf_raw)

n_fill = int((gulf_raw <= FILL_VALUE + 0.1).sum())

gulf_gaps = pd.Series(
    {
        "timesteps in file": len(gulf_raw),
        "listed in missing_time_index": n_missing,
        "equal to the -9 fill": n_fill,
        "timesteps after masking": len(gulf_clean),
        "raw mean Hm0 [m]": round(float(gulf_raw.mean()), 3),
        "masked mean Hm0 [m]": round(float(gulf_clean.Hm0.mean()), 3),
    },
    name="Gulf of Mexico / Puerto Rico, 2010",
)
# Printed, not returned. Quarto's ipynb writer keeps stream output but drops both
# pandas' HTML table repr (blank cell) and IPython Markdown output (no cell at all),
# so `print(...)` is the only form that survives into the notebook deliverable.
print(gulf_gaps.to_string())
```

### The $-90°$ direction correction is not carried over

An earlier version of this example rotated West Coast directions by $-90°$
(`Dir = (Dir - 90) % 360`). That line was written against the **1-hour virtual buoy**, a
different dataset from the 3-hour grid loaded here, and it is **not** applied below. The
reasoning, and its limits, are worth setting out rather than burying, because this is a
judgement call and not a settled fact.

At PacWave South in 2010 the two products disagree. The 3-hour grid's raw directions
concentrate in the W and SW octants (circular mean 276°); the virtual buoy's concentrate in
NW and N (circular mean 354°) — a difference of roughly 80°, close to but not exactly the
90° the old correction assumed. Applied to the 3-hour grid, $-90°$ shifts the modal octant
to S, which would mean the Oregon coast's waves arrive from the south. Left alone, the
3-hour directions are consistent with the WNW prevailing direction quoted in PacWave's site
specification.

That is the whole basis for the decision: **one site, one year, and a site brochure.** It
is enough to conclude the correction should not be blindly inherited by a different
dataset, and not enough to conclude the raw 3-hour values are free of any offset. The
provenance of the original $-90°$ — whether it corrected a genuine convention mismatch or
compensated for something else — is unknown to this document. Anyone depending on absolute
direction here should confirm the convention against the data provider or a co-located
measured buoy before trusting it. Directionality drives Section 7 only; the rest of this
document is unaffected.

## Exploring the Downloaded Data

### Metadata

Each grid node carries a `meta` record: its true position, water depth, distance to shore,
and jurisdiction. Reading **one row** of `meta` is cheap and exactly what it is for; it is
reading all 700k rows to find a node that is not (see `nearest_gid`). Its fields vary by
domain in both name and order — the Gulf calls depth `depth` where the others say
`water_depth` — so the row is unpacked from whatever the file declares rather than from a
fixed list.

```{python}
def read_site_meta_row():
    """The `meta` table row for the selected site's grid node."""
    with Resource(region_path(hindcast_region, YEAR)) as handle:
        return handle.h5["meta"][data.attrs["gid"]]


meta_row = cached(
    f"meta_{hindcast_region}_{data.attrs['gid']}_{YEAR}", read_site_meta_row
)

site_meta = {
    field: (
        value.decode(errors="replace") if isinstance(value, bytes) else value
    )
    for field, value in zip(meta_row.dtype.names, meta_row)
}

metadata = pd.Series(
    {
        "site": SITES[SELECTED_SITE]["label"],
        "domain": hindcast_region,
        "source file": region_path(hindcast_region, YEAR),
        "requested lat, lon": f"{lat_lon[0]:.4f}, {lat_lon[1]:.4f}",
        "nearest node lat, lon": "{:.4f}, {:.4f}".format(*data.attrs["node"]),
        "node index (gid)": data.attrs["gid"],
        "year": data.attrs["year"],
        "sea states": len(data),
        "timestep [s]": int((data.index[1] - data.index[0]).total_seconds()),
        **site_meta,
    },
    name=SELECTED_SITE,
)
# max_colwidth: keep the full S3 URI instead of truncating it to "...West_C...".
with pd.option_context("display.max_colwidth", None):
    print(metadata.to_string())  # see the note on the gap table above
```

### Data Preview

```{python}
print(data.head().to_string())
```

## 1. Aggregate Statistics (percentile)

To calculate aggregate wave energy statistics we are going to use an mhkit function in the wave
module called `performance.statistics`. We can print the docstring of the function using
`inspect.getdoc`

```{python}
print(inspect.getdoc(performance.statistics))
```

Now we plugin in the downloaded `data` into the `performance.statistics` function

```{python}
print(
    pd.DataFrame(
        {q: performance.statistics(data[q]) for q in ["Hm0", "Te", "J"]}
    ).to_string()
)
```

```{python}
print(
    data[["Hm0", "Te", "J"]]
    .describe(percentiles=[0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 0.9, 0.95, 0.99])
    .to_string()
)
```

## 2. Monthly Climatology

Box-and-whisker of the 3-hourly distribution per month, and a daily median with a
25th to 75th percentile intraday band across the year.

```{python}
import calendar

QOIS = {"Hm0": "$H_{m0}$ [m]", "Te": "$T_e$ [s]", "J": "$J$ [kW/m]"}
months = np.arange(1, 13)
month_labels = [calendar.month_abbr[m] for m in months]  # 'Jan'..'Dec'
colors = sns.color_palette()  # default seaborn color cycle

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(8, 8))
for i, (ax, (q, label)) in enumerate(zip(axes, QOIS.items())):
    sns.boxplot(x=data.index.month, y=data[q], ax=ax, color=colors[i])
    ax.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel=label)
    ax.set_xticks(range(12))
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("monthly_boxplot")
```

```{python}
doy = data.index.dayofyear
dgrp = data.groupby(doy)
month_starts = [pd.Timestamp(YEAR, m, 1).dayofyear for m in months]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(8, 8))
for i, (ax, (q, label)) in enumerate(zip(axes, QOIS.items())):
    stats = dgrp[q].describe()
    days = stats.index
    ax.fill_between(
        days,
        stats["25%"],
        stats["75%"],
        color=colors[i],
        alpha=0.3,
        label="Daily 25–75%",
    )
    ax.plot(days, stats["50%"], color=colors[i], label="Daily Median")
    roll = stats["50%"].rolling(7, center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
    ax.plot(days, roll, color="0.4", linewidth=2, label="7-day avg")
    ax.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel=label)
    ax.margins(x=0)
    ax.set_xticks(month_starts)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
    ax.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("daily_climatology")
```

7-day moving average of the daily median, per variable.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(8, 8))
for i, (ax, (q, label)) in enumerate(zip(axes, QOIS.items())):
    roll = dgrp[q].median().rolling(7, center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
    ax.plot(roll.index, roll, color=colors[i], label="7-day avg")
    ax.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel=label)
    ax.margins(x=0)
    ax.set_xticks(month_starts)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("weekly_average")
```

The same average over a 30-day window. A week still tracks individual storm systems; a
month smooths past them and leaves the seasonal signal on its own.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(8, 8))
for i, (ax, (q, label)) in enumerate(zip(axes, QOIS.items())):
    roll = dgrp[q].median().rolling(30, center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
    ax.plot(roll.index, roll, color=colors[i], label="30-day avg")
    ax.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel=label)
    ax.margins(x=0)
    ax.set_xticks(month_starts)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("monthly_average")
```

### Monthly bars: the digestible version

The plots above answer "what does the year look like?" — these answer "how much, in
which month?" One bar per month, labeled, no reading values off an axis.

Mean and 95th percentile are split into separate charts on purpose. They are different
questions and they do not move together: the mean is roughly what a device harvests in an
average month, while the 95th percentile is the energetic tail it has to survive.
Plotting them as paired bars on one axis would let the tail squash the mean flat, and
putting them on two y-axes would be worse.

The $J$ pair is the one to look at. At PacWave South in January the mean is 91 kW/m while
the 95th percentile is 211 — the top 5% of sea states run about 2.3 times the month's
average, and that gap is the central difficulty of wave energy: the resource you earn
from and the load you must survive are not the same number. The ratio is not a winter
story either. It ranges from roughly 1.6 in August to 3.3 in September, so the *peakiest*
months are the shoulder months, when quiet spells are punctuated by the first big swells
rather than sustained by them.

```{python}
def monthly_bar(q, stat_label, agg, fmt, name):
    """One labeled bar per month of `q`, aggregated by `agg`.

    Single series, so a single hue and no legend -- the axis label names it. The
    colour matches this variable's colour in the charts above.
    """
    monthly = data[q].groupby(data.index.month).agg(agg)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
    bars = ax.bar(months, monthly.reindex(months).values,
                  color=colors[list(QOIS).index(q)])
    ax.bar_label(bars, fmt=fmt, padding=2, fontsize=9, color="0.25")
    ax.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel=f"{stat_label} {QOIS[q]}")
    ax.set_xticks(months)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels)
    ax.margins(y=0.14)  # headroom so the tallest bar's label is not clipped
    fig.tight_layout()
    savefig(name)


p95 = lambda s: s.quantile(0.95)

monthly_bar("Hm0", "Mean", "mean", "%.2f", "monthly_bar_hm0_mean")
monthly_bar("Hm0", "95th percentile", p95, "%.2f", "monthly_bar_hm0_p95")
monthly_bar("J", "Mean", "mean", "%.1f", "monthly_bar_j_mean")
monthly_bar("J", "95th percentile", p95, "%.1f", "monthly_bar_j_p95")
```

## 3. Joint Probability Distribution

Joint probability distribution (percent of the year) over the ($H_{m0}$, $T_e$)
sea-state bins.

```{python}
Hm0_bins = np.arange(0, np.ceil(data.Hm0.max()) + 0.5, 0.5)
Te_bins = np.arange(0, np.ceil(data.Te.max()) + 1, 1)

# Joint probability of occurrence (reused as the AEP frequency matrix below).
frequency = performance.wave_energy_flux_matrix(
    data.Hm0, data.Te, data.J, "frequency", Hm0_bins, Te_bins
)
print(frequency.to_string())
```

```{python}
# Percent of the year each sea state occurs (frequency sums to 1 -> ×100 = %).
frequency_pct = frequency * 100

plot_wave_matrix(
    frequency_pct, zlabel="Joint Probability [% of year]"
)


savefig("jpd_frequency_matrix")
```

## 4. Extreme Sea States

Fifty- and one-hundred-year environmental contours bound the extreme sea states a device
must survive. `contours.environmental_contours` offers **ten** methods, in three families:
five parametric (`PCA`, `gaussian`, `gumbel`, `clayton`, `rosenblatt`), three nonparametric
(`nonparametric_gaussian`, `nonparametric_clayton`, `nonparametric_gumbel`), and two
kernel-density (`bivariate_KDE`, `bivariate_KDE_log`).

Everything below uses `PCA`, following the Wave Energy Converter Design Response Toolbox
(WDRT). It applies principal component analysis to $(H_{m0}, T_e)$, fits an inverse Gaussian
in the rotated frame, and reads the contour off a circle of iso-probability using the
inverse first-order reliability method (I-FORM) — Eckert-Gallup et al. 2016.

**The choice is not a detail.** Running this same year of PacWave South data through all ten
puts the 100-year $H_{m0}$ anywhere between **8.2 m and 16.5 m — it doubles**. The four
parametric copulas agree exactly with each other (8.2 m) because they share one marginal
Weibull fit for $H_{m0}$; the copula only shapes the conditional $T_e$, which itself ranges
from 11.0 to 20.2 s at the contour's peak. The three nonparametric methods likewise agree
(8.6 m). So there are really four distinct answers: 8.2, 8.6, 11.2 (PCA), and 16.5
(`bivariate_KDE_log`).

The 11.2 m reported below is therefore *PCA's* answer, not *the* answer, and the method
matters more than anything else varied in this document — more than the site, and more than
the length of the record. Read "a single hindcast year is illustrative, not a formal
extreme-value estimate" as meaning it literally.

Eckert-Gallup, A. C., Sallaberry, C. J., Dallman, A. R., & Neary, V. S. (2016). Application
of principal component analysis (PCA) and improved joint probability distributions to the
inverse first-order reliability method (I-FORM) for predicting extreme sea states. *Ocean
Engineering*, 112, 307–319.

Coe, Ryan, Michelen, Carlos, Eckert-Gallup, Aubrey, Yu, Yi-Hsiang, and Van Rij, Jennifer.
(2016, March 30). WEC Design Response Toolbox v. 1.0 (Version 00) [Computer software].
https://www.osti.gov//servlets/purl/1312743.

```{python}
# Seconds between sea-state samples (3-hour hindcast -> 10800 s).
dt = (data.index[1] - data.index[0]).total_seconds()

copulas50 = contours.environmental_contours(data.Hm0, data.Te, dt, 50, method="PCA")
copulas100 = contours.environmental_contours(data.Hm0, data.Te, dt, 100, method="PCA")

Hm0_contours = [copulas50["PCA_x1"], copulas100["PCA_x1"]]
Te_contours = [copulas50["PCA_x2"], copulas100["PCA_x2"]]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
graphics.plot_environmental_contour(
    data.Te.values,
    data.Hm0.values,
    Te_contours,
    Hm0_contours,
    data_label=SITES[SELECTED_SITE]["label"],
    contour_label=["50-year contour", "100-year contour"],
    x_label="Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]",
    y_label="Significant Wave Height, $H_{m0}$ [m]",
    ax=ax,
)
ax.legend(loc="upper left")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("extreme_contours")
```

```{python}
# Peak of each contour: the largest H_m0 and the T_e it occurs at.
for label, cop in (("50-year", copulas50), ("100-year", copulas100)):
    i = cop["PCA_x1"].argmax()
    print(f"{label}: H_m0 max {cop['PCA_x1'].max():.1f} m at T_e {cop['PCA_x2'][i]:.1f} s")
```

## 5. Representative Sea State Clusters

Simulating a wave energy converter against every 3-hourly sea state is intractable, so WEC
design optimization uses a limited number of *representative* sea states. A Gaussian-mixture
model (GMM), a more general form of k-means, groups the ($T_e$, $H_{m0}$) sea states into
clusters and locates a representative center for each, following the density of the data.
We fit increasing cluster counts (4, 8, 16, 32, 64); more clusters capture the resource
more faithfully at the cost of more simulations.

The source buoy example next reconstructed each cluster's energy from a JONSWAP spectrum to
compare against the total resource energy. That step needs spectral frequency bins, which the
bulk-parameter hindcast does not provide, so it is omitted here.

```{python}
cluster_counts = [8, 32]
X = np.vstack((data.Te.values, data.Hm0.values)).T

fig, axs = plt.subplots(len(cluster_counts), 1, figsize=(8, 10), sharex=True)
for ax, n in zip(axs, cluster_counts):
    # random_state fixes the fit so renders are reproducible.
    gmm = GaussianMixture(n_components=n, random_state=0).fit(X)
    labels = gmm.predict(X)
    centers = gmm.means_  # columns: Te, Hm0
    ax.scatter(data.Te.values, data.Hm0.values, c=labels, s=20, cmap="viridis")
    ax.plot(centers[:, 0], centers[:, 1], "m+", markersize=12)
    ax.set(title=f"{n} clusters", ylabel="$H_{m0}$ [m]")
axs[-1].set_xlabel("Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("sea_state_clusters")
```

## 6. Exceedance Probability

Empirical survival function $P(H_{m0} > x)$.

```{python}
hm0_sorted = np.sort(data.Hm0.values)
exceedance = 1.0 - np.arange(1, hm0_sorted.size + 1) / (hm0_sorted.size + 1)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 5))
ax.semilogy(hm0_sorted, exceedance)
for pct in (0.99, 1.0):
    val = np.quantile(data.Hm0, pct) if pct < 1.0 else data.Hm0.max()
    ax.axvline(val, ls="--", color="0.5")
    ax.text(val, 1e-3, f"{'max' if pct == 1.0 else '99%'}\n{val:.1f} m", ha="center")
ax.set(xlabel="$H_{m0}$ [m]", ylabel="$P(H_{m0} > x)$")
ax.grid(True, which="both", alpha=0.3)
savefig("exceedance_hm0")
```

## 7. Directionality

Direction (θ) vs $H_{m0}$, $T_e$, and wave power, colored by joint probability
distribution (% of year); plus mean wave power by direction sector.

```{python}
Dir_bins = np.arange(0, 360 + 15, 15)


def polar_jpd(ax, radius, r_bins, r_label):
    counts, _, _ = np.histogram2d(data.Dir, radius, bins=[Dir_bins, r_bins])
    counts = counts / counts.sum() * 100  # % of year per (direction, radius) bin
    th, rr = np.meshgrid(np.deg2rad(Dir_bins), r_bins, indexing="ij")
    pcm = ax.pcolormesh(th, rr, counts, shading="auto", cmap="viridis")
    ax.set_theta_zero_location("N")
    ax.set_theta_direction(-1)
    ax.set_title(r_label, pad=15)
    plt.colorbar(pcm, ax=ax, label="% of year", pad=0.1)


fig, axes = plt.subplots(
    3, 1, figsize=(6, 15), subplot_kw={"projection": "polar"}
)
polar_jpd(axes[0], data.Hm0, Hm0_bins, "$H_{m0}$ [m] by direction")
polar_jpd(axes[1], data.Te, Te_bins, "$T_e$ [s] by direction")
polar_jpd(axes[2], data.J, np.linspace(0, data.J.max(), len(Hm0_bins)), "$J$ [kW/m] by direction")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("directionality_polar_jpd")
```

```{python}
# Mean wave power per direction sector.
sector = pd.cut(data.Dir, Dir_bins)
mean_J = data.J.groupby(sector, observed=False).mean()
centers = np.deg2rad(Dir_bins[:-1] + 7.5)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6, 6), subplot_kw={"projection": "polar"})
ax.bar(centers, mean_J.fillna(0).values, width=np.deg2rad(15), alpha=0.7)
ax.set_theta_zero_location("N")
ax.set_theta_direction(-1)
ax.set_title("Mean $J$ [kW/m] by direction", pad=15)
savefig("directionality_mean_power")
```

## 8. Multiple Sites: Comparison

The single-site pipeline above characterizes one location; here we place several sites
side by side. Edit `SITES_TO_COMPARE`, a plain list of `SITES` keys, to add or omit
sites. `load_site` caches each site to `.cache/`, so re-runs skip S3 entirely. Every site
keeps one color (from Matplotlib's `tab10`) across all four figures.

Reading the source grid rather than the 1-hour virtual buoy widens the comparison from
one domain to five. The sites below span every domain this document reaches — West Coast,
Hawaii, Atlantic, Alaska, and Gulf of Mexico / Puerto Rico — and each is loaded from the
latest version published for *its* domain, which is `v1.0.1` for four of them and `v1.0.0`
for Hawaii. The virtual buoy could not have done this: it covers the West Coast only.

Note what is being compared. The Alaska and Puerto Rico points are open water with no test
facility, included to exercise those domains. The Atlantic is represented by Pioneer WEC
rather than Jennette's Pier, and the reason is worth being precise about, because it is
easy to get wrong.

With MHKiT's **default** binning, the pier's 100-year contour fails outright:
`environmental_contours` raises `LinAlgError: SVD did not converge`. It is tempting to read
that as "the site is too depth-limited to characterise". It is not. `_copula_parameters`
puts everything below `initial_bin_max_val` (default 1.0 m) in one bin and then steps up in
`bin_val_size` (default 0.25 m) increments until a bin holds fewer than `min_bin_count`
(default 40) points. The pier sits in roughly 10 m of water with $H_{m0}$ spanning
0.13–1.62 m, so **1,529 of its 2,920 sea states land in that first bin** and barely 0.6 m of
range is left for the rest. The conditional fit ends up with a handful of nearly identical
$H_{m0}$ values, its quadratic design matrix $(1, x, x^2)$ goes collinear, and the least
squares solve gives up.

Pass `bin_val_size=0.10` and the same site contours without complaint, peaking at 2.89 m.
So the default failure is a statement about MHKiT's bin defaults, tuned for open-ocean
$H_{m0}$ ranges, and not a statement about the ocean at Nags Head. The defaults are a
starting point, not a law. Pioneer WEC is used below simply because it needs no such
tuning; uncomment the pier and pass `bin_val_size=0.10` to see it work.

```{python}
SITES_TO_COMPARE = [
    "US_Oregon_PacWave_South",                 # West_Coast
    "US_Oregon_PacWave_North",                 # West_Coast
    "US_California_Humboldt_Bay",              # West_Coast
    "US_California_San_Luis_Obispo",           # West_Coast
    "US_Hawaii_Oahu_WETS",                     # Hawaii
    "US_Massachusetts_WoodsHole_PioneerWec",   # Atlantic
    "US_Alaska_Kodiak",                        # Alaska
    "US_PuertoRico_North_Shore",               # Gulf_of_Mexico_and_Puerto_Rico
    # "US_North_Carolina_Jenettes_Pier",       # Atlantic; needs bin_val_size=0.10
    #                                          # to contour -- see the note above
]  # comment a key in/out to include/omit that site

# Check the whole set up front. The loop below tolerates a site failing -- that is
# deliberate, for sites outside their domain's grid -- but a cold cache with no
# network would trip that same handler for every site and quietly produce a
# comparison built on whatever survived.
require_cached(
    [CACHE_DIR / f"{name}_{YEAR}.pkl" for name in SITES_TO_COMPARE],
    "Multi-site comparison",
)

site_data = {}
for name in SITES_TO_COMPARE:
    try:
        site_data[name] = load_site(name)
    except Exception as e:  # e.g. a site outside its domain's grid
        print(f"skipping {name}: {type(e).__name__}: {e}")

# One color per site, shared across every figure below.
tab10 = plt.get_cmap("tab10")
site_colors = {name: tab10(i) for i, name in enumerate(site_data)}

{name: df.shape for name, df in site_data.items()}
```

### 7-day moving averages

A 7-day centered moving average of each 3-hourly quantity smooths out storm-scale noise
and exposes the seasonal cycle. One line per site; the legend sits outside the axes.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(9, 9), sharex=True)
for ax, (q, ylabel) in zip(axes, QOIS.items()):
    for name, df in site_data.items():
        roll = df[q].rolling("7D", center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
        ax.plot(roll.index, roll, color=site_colors[name],
                label=SITES[name]["label"], linewidth=1.5)
    ax.set(ylabel=ylabel)
    ax.margins(x=0)
axes[-1].set_xlabel("Date")
axes[0].legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
fig.autofmt_xdate()  # rotate/thin the date ticks; unrotated they overlap into mush
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_moving_average")
```

### 30-day moving averages

The same comparison over a 30-day window. Smoothing past individual storm systems leaves
the seasonal cycle, and the sites do not rank the way latitude alone would suggest.
Comparing each site's mean $J$ over Dec–Feb against Jun–Aug for 2010, the West Coast swings
hardest — PacWave South runs 71 kW/m in winter against 14 kW/m in summer, a factor of 5 —
while Hawaii is the flattest at about 2. But Kodiak, the highest-latitude site here, is
mid-pack at roughly 2.9, *less* seasonal than the Puerto Rico point at 3.6 and than San
Luis Obispo at 4.5. Seasonality here is set by which storm tracks a site is exposed to,
not by how far north it sits. As ever, these are one year's numbers.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(9, 9), sharex=True)
for ax, (q, ylabel) in zip(axes, QOIS.items()):
    for name, df in site_data.items():
        roll = df[q].rolling("30D", center=True, min_periods=1).mean()
        ax.plot(roll.index, roll, color=site_colors[name],
                label=SITES[name]["label"], linewidth=1.5)
    ax.set(ylabel=ylabel)
    ax.margins(x=0)
axes[-1].set_xlabel("Date")
axes[0].legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
fig.autofmt_xdate()  # rotate/thin the date ticks; unrotated they overlap into mush
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_moving_average_30d")
```

### Monthly grids: every site, every month

The single-site bars do not survive being repeated eight times: mean $J$ spans 91 kW/m at
PacWave South in January down to 2 kW/m at San Luis Obispo in August, a factor of about
45, so a shared axis flattens the low-energy sites into slivers and eight sets of grouped
bars is 96 bars nobody can read. A grid takes the same numbers and gives each site a row.
Every cell is labeled, so the exact value is still there to read — colour is the fast path,
not the only one.

Rows are ordered by annual mean $J$, so the ranking reads straight down the y-axis.

```{python}
# Sites ranked by annual mean J, richest first. Every figure below reuses this order so
# the ranking is the same wherever you look. Colour still follows the site, not its rank.
site_order = sorted(site_data, key=lambda n: site_data[n]["J"].mean(), reverse=True)


def site_month_matrix(q, agg):
    """Sites (rows) x months (columns) of `q` aggregated by `agg`, richest site first."""
    frame = pd.DataFrame(
        {
            SITES[name]["label"]: site_data[name][q]
            .groupby(site_data[name].index.month)
            .agg(agg)
            .reindex(months)
            for name in site_order
        }
    ).T
    frame.columns = month_labels
    return frame


def plot_site_month_heatmap(matrix, cbar_label, name, fmt=".0f", **kw):
    """Annotated sites x months grid. Values are labeled, so colour need not be exact."""
    plt.figure(figsize=(11, 4.5))
    ax = sns.heatmap(matrix, annot=True, fmt=fmt, annot_kws={"size": 8},
                     linewidths=0.5, cbar_kws={"label": cbar_label}, **kw)
    ax.set_xlabel("Month")
    ax.tick_params(axis="y", rotation=0)
    plt.tight_layout()
    savefig(name)
```

The dynamic range is the point of this first pair, so the colour scale is absolute and
shared: the low-energy rows *should* look dark next to PacWave, because that is the
finding. Mean and 95th percentile stay on separate grids for the same reason they were
split for the single site.

```{python}
plot_site_month_heatmap(
    site_month_matrix("J", "mean"),
    "Mean $J$ [kW/m]", "multisite_month_j_mean", cmap="viridis",
)
plot_site_month_heatmap(
    site_month_matrix("J", p95),
    "95th percentile $J$ [kW/m]", "multisite_month_j_p95", cmap="viridis",
)
```

The same means, now divided by each site's own annual mean. This deliberately throws away
magnitude to ask a different question — *when* is each site's season? — and it surfaces
structure the absolute grid cannot show, because a site can be both small and violently
seasonal. Red is a month above that site's annual average, blue below, white at 1.0.

```{python}
j_mean = site_month_matrix("J", "mean")
plot_site_month_heatmap(
    j_mean.div(j_mean.mean(axis=1), axis=0),
    "$J$ relative to that site's annual mean", "multisite_month_j_seasonality",
    fmt=".1f", cmap="RdBu_r", center=1.0,
)
```

### Four other ways to look at the same numbers

A grid is a table wearing colour: with every cell labeled, the colour earns little, and a
monthly mean has already thrown away the distribution underneath it. Below are four
different framings of the same eight sites. None of them is strictly better — each buys
legibility by giving something up, and the give-up is named in each case. The tension is
always the same: **no linear, month-resolved view can hold a 45× spread across eight
sites**, so every option drops months, drops magnitude, or distorts the scale.

#### Mean against 95th percentile

The most compact of the four, and the one that concedes least. Both statistics sit on a
single linear axis, so the spread is real rather than transformed, and the **gap** — not
either endpoint — is the subject. That gap is the design problem: PacWave South earns from
a 46 kW/m average but must survive 157, while San Luis Obispo lives between 6 and 18. What
it gives up is the calendar: there are no months here at all.

```{python}
annual_mean = pd.Series({n: site_data[n]["J"].mean() for n in site_order})
annual_p95 = pd.Series({n: site_data[n]["J"].quantile(0.95) for n in site_order})
rank = annual_mean.sort_values().index  # ascending: richest at the top of the axis

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.5))
y = np.arange(len(rank))
ax.hlines(y, annual_mean[rank], annual_p95[rank], color="0.75", linewidth=2, zorder=1)
ax.scatter(annual_mean[rank], y, s=70, zorder=2,
           color=[site_colors[n] for n in rank], label="annual mean")
ax.scatter(annual_p95[rank], y, s=70, marker="D", facecolors="none", zorder=2,
           edgecolors=[site_colors[n] for n in rank], label="95th percentile")
for i, n in enumerate(rank):
    for value in (annual_mean[n], annual_p95[n]):
        ax.annotate(f"{value:.0f}", (value, i), xytext=(0, 8),
                    textcoords="offset points", ha="center", size=7, color="0.25")
ax.set_yticks(y)
ax.set_yticklabels([SITES[n]["label"] for n in rank])
ax.set_xlabel("$J$ [kW/m]")
ax.margins(x=0.08, y=0.09)
ax.legend(loc="lower right")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_mean_vs_p95")
```

#### Winter against summer

The seasonal collapse as a single stroke per site. Steeper is more seasonal. It keeps a
true linear axis and needs no legend, but compresses the year into two numbers, so a site
whose peak falls outside DJF — Pioneer WEC's September, say — is misrepresented by it.

```{python}
winter = pd.Series({n: site_data[n]["J"][site_data[n].index.month.isin([12, 1, 2])].mean()
                    for n in site_order})
summer = pd.Series({n: site_data[n]["J"][site_data[n].index.month.isin([6, 7, 8])].mean()
                    for n in site_order})

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.5, 6))
def spread(values):
    """Label y-positions with a minimum gap, so co-located sites don't overprint.

    Several sites share a value at each end -- WETS and San Luis Obispo are both
    ~11 kW/m in winter, PacWave South and North both ~14 in summer -- and their
    labels would otherwise sit on top of each other.
    """
    gap = (values.max() - values.min()) * 0.035
    out, last = {}, -np.inf
    for n in values.sort_values().index:
        out[n] = last = max(values[n], last + gap)
    return out


winter_y, summer_y = spread(winter), spread(summer)
for n in site_order:
    ax.plot([0, 1], [winter[n], summer[n]], marker="o", color=site_colors[n], linewidth=2)
    ax.annotate(f"{SITES[n]['label']}  {winter[n]:.0f}", (0, winter_y[n]),
                ha="right", va="center", size=8, color="0.25",
                xytext=(-8, 0), textcoords="offset points")
    ax.annotate(f"{summer[n]:.0f}", (1, summer_y[n]), ha="left", va="center", size=8,
                color="0.25", xytext=(8, 0), textcoords="offset points")
ax.set_xlim(-0.62, 1.22)
ax.set_xticks([0, 1])
ax.set_xticklabels(["Winter (DJF)", "Summer (JJA)"])
ax.set_ylabel("Mean $J$ [kW/m]")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_winter_summer")
```

#### Small multiples

The single-site monthly bars, repeated per site on one shared linear axis. An earlier
version of this figure used a log axis so that every site's seasonal shape stayed legible.
That was a mistake worth naming: a log axis turns PacWave's 45-fold advantage over San Luis
Obispo into about one gridline, which *hides the very thing this comparison exists to
show*. Wave power spans decades honestly, and squashing it to fit is not a rendering
convenience, it is a misrepresentation.

So the axis is linear and the bottom row is nearly flat. That is not a defect — San Luis
Obispo really does have a fortieth of PacWave's resource, and any chart that lets you
forget it is lying. Take seasonal shape from the seasonality grid, which normalises
explicitly and says so.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(13, 5.4), sharey=True)
for ax, name in zip(axes.flat, site_order):
    monthly = site_data[name]["J"].groupby(site_data[name].index.month).mean().reindex(months)
    ax.bar(months, monthly.values, color=site_colors[name])
    ax.set_title(SITES[name]["label"], size=9)
    ax.set_xticks(months)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels, size=6, rotation=90)
    ax.grid(True, which="both", axis="y", alpha=0.3)
for ax in axes[:, 0]:
    ax.set_ylabel("Mean $J$ [kW/m]")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_month_bars")
```

#### Polar

Months are cyclical and a circle says so: December sits next to January, which every
chart above breaks. The radial scale is shared, so the polygons are honestly comparable —
but that is also the catch, as the low-energy sites shrink to specks, and the eye reads
polar *area*, which grows as the square of the radius and so overstates the big sites.
Best treated as a shape-recognition view, not a measurement one.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 4, figsize=(13, 6.6), subplot_kw={"projection": "polar"})
theta = np.linspace(0, 2 * np.pi, 12, endpoint=False)
radial_max = max(
    site_data[n]["J"].groupby(site_data[n].index.month).mean().max() for n in site_order
)
for ax, name in zip(axes.flat, site_order):
    monthly = site_data[name]["J"].groupby(site_data[name].index.month).mean().reindex(months)
    closed_r = np.append(monthly.values, monthly.values[0])  # close the polygon
    closed_t = np.append(theta, theta[0])
    ax.plot(closed_t, closed_r, color=site_colors[name], linewidth=2)
    ax.fill(closed_t, closed_r, color=site_colors[name], alpha=0.25)
    ax.set_xticks(theta)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels, size=6)
    ax.set_theta_zero_location("N")
    ax.set_theta_direction(-1)  # clockwise: Jan -> Feb -> Mar
    ax.set_ylim(0, radial_max)
    ax.set_title(SITES[name]["label"], size=9, pad=12)
    ax.tick_params(labelsize=5)
fig.suptitle("Mean $J$ [kW/m], shared radial scale", size=10)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_month_polar")
```

### Three more, from seaborn's own gallery

Everything above compares *monthly means*, which is the real complaint against the grid:
a mean is one number standing in for 240 sea states, and the spread it hides is most of
what distinguishes these sites. Seaborn's figure-level plots (`relplot`, `catplot`) take
the same data in long form and put that spread back.

```{python}
# Long ("tidy") form: one row per sea state per site, which is what the figure-level
# seaborn functions expect. Site is an *ordered categorical* -- the ranking has to live
# in the data, not in a set_yticklabels() call, or the axis silently disagrees with the
# marks it is labelling.
site_labels = [SITES[n]["label"] for n in site_order]
tidy = pd.concat(
    [site_data[n].assign(Site=SITES[n]["label"]) for n in site_order]
)
tidy["MonthNum"] = tidy.index.month
tidy["Month"] = pd.Categorical(
    [month_labels[m - 1] for m in tidy.index.month], categories=month_labels, ordered=True
)
tidy["Site"] = pd.Categorical(tidy["Site"], categories=site_labels, ordered=True)
label_colors = {SITES[n]["label"]: site_colors[n] for n in site_order}
```

#### `relplot`: month profiles with their spread

The one that concedes least of anything here. A shared **linear** axis keeps the dynamic
range honest — San Luis Obispo really is a flat line next to PacWave, and that is the
finding, not a rendering artifact — while the band spans the 25th–75th percentile of the
sea states behind each monthly point, so the mean is no longer alone. The cost is only
vertical space.

```{python}
g = sns.relplot(
    data=tidy, x="MonthNum", y="J", col="Site", col_order=site_labels, col_wrap=4,
    kind="line", errorbar=("pi", 50), height=2.5, aspect=1.5, color="#4c72b0",
)
for ax in g.axes.flat:
    ax.set_xticks(months)
    ax.set_xticklabels(month_labels, size=6, rotation=90)
    ax.set_xlabel("")
g.set_titles("{col_name}", size=9)
g.set_ylabels("$J$ [kW/m]")
g.figure.suptitle("Monthly mean $J$ with 25th–75th percentile band", y=1.02, size=10)
savefig("multisite_relplot_month_band")
```

#### `relplot`: magnitude as size, not just colour

The grid's failing was that colour saturates — every low-energy row goes uniformly dark
and stops carrying information. Encoding the same number as **area as well as hue** gives
it somewhere else to go, and the 45× spread becomes visible as size while the calendar
stays on the x-axis. Same numbers as the first heatmap, read far more easily.

```{python}
monthly_mean = (
    tidy.groupby(["Site", "Month"], observed=True)["J"].mean().reset_index()
)
g = sns.relplot(
    data=monthly_mean, x="Month", y="Site", size="J", hue="J",
    sizes=(15, 420), palette="viridis", height=4.2, aspect=2.3, legend="brief",
)
g.set(xlabel="Month", ylabel="")
g.ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
g.figure.suptitle("Mean $J$ [kW/m] — area and hue both carry magnitude", y=1.02, size=10)
savefig("multisite_relplot_bubbles")
```

#### `catplot`: the whole distribution, not a mean

The most detailed view, and the direct answer to "a monthly mean is too coarse". Each box
is the full distribution of that month's sea states — median, quartiles, and the
progressively thinner tails a letter-value plot draws instead of lumping them into
"outliers". Look at Pioneer WEC in September: the month averages 47 kW/m but its tail
reaches **895 kW/m**, nineteen times its own mean. That is a hurricane, it is the single
most energetic thing in this entire comparison, and *every* mean-based chart above —
including all three heatmaps — renders it as an unremarkable 47. The axis is linear, so
that tail is drawn at its true distance from the body of the distribution, and the calmer
sites' boxes are correspondingly small. That is the honest shape of wave power.

```{python}
g = sns.catplot(
    data=tidy, x="Month", y="J", col="Site", col_order=site_labels, col_wrap=4,
    kind="boxen", height=2.5, aspect=1.5, color="#4c72b0", linewidth=0.4,
)
for ax in g.axes.flat:
    ax.set_xlabel("")
    ax.tick_params(axis="x", labelsize=6, rotation=90)
g.set_titles("{col_name}", size=9)
g.set_ylabels("$J$ [kW/m]")
savefig("multisite_catplot_boxen")
```

### 100-year contours

The 100-year environmental contours from Section 4, overlaid across sites: each site's
3-hourly sea states appear as a light scatter, with its PCA 100-year contour drawn in the
same color. As in Section 4, a single hindcast year is illustrative, not a formal
extreme-value estimate.

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 6))
for name, df in site_data.items():
    color = site_colors[name]
    dt = (df.index[1] - df.index[0]).total_seconds()  # 10800 s for 3-hour data
    ax.scatter(df.Te, df.Hm0, s=4, color=color, alpha=0.15, edgecolors="none")
    try:
        cop = contours.environmental_contours(df.Hm0, df.Te, dt, 100, method="PCA")
    except np.linalg.LinAlgError as e:
        # A narrow Hm0 range can collapse the conditional fit's design matrix under
        # the default binning (see the note above; bin_val_size=0.10 usually fixes
        # it). Show the site's scatter, skip its contour, and say which and why.
        print(f"no contour for {SITES[name]['label']}: {e}")
        continue
    ax.plot(cop["PCA_x2"], cop["PCA_x1"], color=color, linewidth=2.2,
            label=SITES[name]["label"])
ax.set(
    xlabel="Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]",
    ylabel="Significant Wave Height, $H_{m0}$ [m]",
)
ax.legend(title="100-year contour", loc="upper left",
          bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_contour_100yr")
```

## 9. Direct Comparison: One Axes, All Sites

Everything in Section 8 that used facets asks you to hold eight panels in your head and
compare from memory. The contour figure above does not — every site is drawn on one pair
of axes, so the comparison happens in the eye rather than the head. That is what makes it
the most useful figure in this document, and it works because each site is reduced to *a
curve*: eight curves coexist on one axes where eight histograms cannot.

The charts below apply that same idea to the rest of the comparison. Each is one axes and
all eight sites.

### The scatter layer is doing no work

The contour figure layers ~23,000 sea states under the curves, and eight overlapping
clouds average out to a brown haze — the *contours* carry that chart, not the points. A
2-D kernel density draws the same information as one closed curve per site: the 50%
iso-density envelope, inside which each site spends half its year.

This is where each site actually lives. San Luis Obispo is the outlier — long-period,
low-height swell, sitting clearly apart from everything else — while PacWave South, North
and Humboldt Bay overlap almost exactly, which is the real finding: they are effectively
the same sea state, differing in how much of it they get.

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 6))
sns.kdeplot(
    data=tidy, x="Te", y="Hm0", hue="Site", hue_order=site_labels,
    palette=label_colors, levels=[0.5], linewidths=2, ax=ax,
)
ax.set(xlabel="Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]",
       ylabel="Significant Wave Height, $H_{m0}$ [m]", ylim=(0, 4))
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_operating_envelope")
```

### Operating envelope against 100-year extreme

The contour figure above is left as it is; this is the same idea with the scatter replaced
by the envelope. Dashed is where a site spends half its year, solid is the 100-year
contour it must survive, and the **distance between them is the engineering problem**:
PacWave South operates around $H_{m0}$ 2–2.5 m and is designed against 11 m. The envelopes
look small precisely because the extremes are so far away — that gap is the subject, not a
scaling accident.

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9.5, 7))
sns.kdeplot(  # operational: 50% of sea states
    data=tidy, x="Te", y="Hm0", hue="Site", hue_order=site_labels,
    palette=label_colors, levels=[0.5], linewidths=1.6, linestyles="--",
    ax=ax, legend=False,
)
for name in site_order:  # extreme: the same 100-year contours as above
    df = site_data[name]
    dt = (df.index[1] - df.index[0]).total_seconds()
    try:
        cop = contours.environmental_contours(df.Hm0, df.Te, dt, 100, method="PCA")
    except np.linalg.LinAlgError:
        continue
    ax.plot(cop["PCA_x2"], cop["PCA_x1"], color=site_colors[name], linewidth=2.2,
            label=SITES[name]["label"])
ax.set(xlabel="Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]",
       ylabel="Significant Wave Height, $H_{m0}$ [m]", ylim=(0, 16))
ax.legend(title="Site", loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_envelope_vs_contour")
```

### Exceedance: how much of the year, above what

The most direct answer to "which site has more resource, and how much more". Every sea
state of the year enters the curve — nothing is binned, smoothed, or averaged away — and
the curves nest left to right, so the ranking *is* the picture. Read any threshold
straight off: at 50 kW/m, PacWave South spends 29.5% of the year above it and San Luis
Obispo 0.4%, a difference of roughly seventy times that no bar chart of means conveys.

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 5.5))
sns.ecdfplot(
    data=tidy, x="J", hue="Site", hue_order=site_labels, palette=label_colors,
    complementary=True, linewidth=2, ax=ax,
)
for fraction in (0.5, 0.1):  # median and top-decile reference lines
    ax.axhline(fraction, color="0.6", linestyle=":", linewidth=1)
ax.set(xlabel="$J$ [kW/m]", ylabel="Fraction of the year exceeding")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_exceedance_all")
```

### Ranked distributions

The same distributions as violins, ranked by annual mean, with quartiles marked inside.
Pioneer WEC is the shape to notice: the tallest violin of the eight despite a middling
average, spanning 0.15 to 895 kW/m because a hurricane sits in its tail.

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(9, 5))
sns.violinplot(
    data=tidy, x="Site", y="J", order=site_labels, hue="Site", hue_order=site_labels,
    palette=label_colors, legend=False, density_norm="width",
    cut=0, inner="quartile", ax=ax,
)
ax.set(xlabel="", ylabel="$J$ [kW/m]")
ax.tick_params(axis="x", rotation=25)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_violin_ranked")
```

### Envelope with marginals

The envelope again, with each site's $T_e$ and $H_{m0}$ distributions on the margins —
the most information of any figure here. The marginals are what earn it: the $T_e$ ridge
along the top separates the sites more cleanly than the joint panel does, and it is where
San Luis Obispo's long-period swell and Pioneer WEC's short, steep seas are most obvious.

```{python}
grid = sns.JointGrid(data=tidy, x="Te", y="Hm0", hue="Site", hue_order=site_labels,
                     palette=label_colors, height=7)
grid.plot_joint(sns.kdeplot, levels=[0.5], linewidths=1.8)
grid.plot_marginals(sns.kdeplot, fill=False, linewidth=1.5, common_norm=False)
grid.ax_joint.set_ylim(0, 4)
grid.set_axis_labels("Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]", "Significant Wave Height, $H_{m0}$ [m]")
grid.ax_joint.legend_.set(bbox_to_anchor=(1.28, 1), loc="upper left", title="Site")
savefig("multisite_jointgrid")
```

### The raw distributions

Everything up to here has compared a *statistic* — a mean, a percentile, an envelope, an
extreme. These are the underlying distributions themselves, all eight sites on one axes
per variable, nothing summarised, and every axis linear — $J$ spans decades and is drawn
that way, with its long right tail at full length rather than folded up to fit.

$H_{m0}$ and $T_e$ are drawn as densities; $J$ is not, and the reason is worth stating. A
density of $J$ on a linear axis is a spike against the origin: one 895 kW/m hurricane sets
the axis while 99% of the year lives in the first few percent of it. That is the honest
shape and it is also unreadable. Squeezing the axis to fix it would misrepresent the
spread, so $J$ gets an empirical CDF instead — every sea state exactly as recorded, no
bandwidth guess, nothing truncated. Read it as "what fraction of the year sits below this
power".

$T_e$ is the surprise. The sites barely separate in $H_{m0}$ — every one of them piles up
between 1 and 3 m — and they overlap heavily in $J$ too. But in $T_e$ they fall into a
clean ordered sequence, from Pioneer WEC's short, steep wind sea near 4.5 s through to San
