Luis Obispo's long Pacific swell near 12 s. Period, not height, is what actually
distinguishes these sites from one another, and it is the variable a device is tuned to.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(3, 1, figsize=(8.5, 9.5))
for i, (ax, (q, label)) in enumerate(zip(axes, QOIS.items())):
    if q == "J":  # too heavy-tailed for a density on a linear axis
        sns.ecdfplot(data=tidy, x=q, hue="Site", hue_order=site_labels,
                     palette=label_colors, linewidth=1.8, ax=ax, legend=(i == 0))
        ax.set(xlabel=label, ylabel="Proportion below")
    else:
        sns.kdeplot(data=tidy, x=q, hue="Site", hue_order=site_labels,
                    palette=label_colors, linewidth=1.8, common_norm=False,
                    ax=ax, legend=(i == 0))
        ax.set(xlabel=label, ylabel="Density")
    if i == 0:
        sns.move_legend(ax, "upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), title="Site")
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_distributions")
```

### The same distributions, month by month

`displot` is the distribution-flavoured sibling of `relplot` — same FacetGrid underneath,
so `col="Month", col_wrap=3` lays the year out three across and four down. Each panel is
one month with all eight sites overlaid, which turns the seasonal cycle into something you
watch rather than infer: the whole field slides left into August and marches back right by
December, and the sites' *ordering* barely changes while doing it.

The $H_{m0}$ and $T_e$ y-axes are unshared and unlabeled deliberately: density height
depends on how narrow a distribution is, so comparing peak heights across panels would
mislead — shape and horizontal position are the signal. $J$ is an ECDF for the reason given
above, and its y-axis *is* meaningful.

```{python}
for q, label in QOIS.items():
    g = sns.displot(
        data=tidy, x=q, hue="Site", hue_order=site_labels, palette=label_colors,
        col="Month", col_wrap=3, kind="ecdf" if q == "J" else "kde",
        height=2.0, aspect=1.5, linewidth=1.2, facet_kws={"sharey": False},
    )
    g.set_titles("{col_name}", size=9)
    g.set_axis_labels(label, "Proportion below" if q == "J" else "")
    if q != "J":
        g.set(yticks=[])
    g.figure.suptitle(f"{label} distribution by month, all sites", y=1.01, size=11)
    savefig(f"multisite_dist_by_month_{q.lower()}")
```

### Each site against the field

Borrowed from seaborn's [`timeseries_facets`](https://seaborn.pydata.org/examples/timeseries_facets.html)
example, whose real trick is not the faceting — it is that every panel redraws the *whole
field* in grey and highlights one series on top. That is what repairs the usual failing of
small multiples: each panel becomes a comparison in itself rather than something you have
to hold in memory while looking at the next one.

It only works with **one** highlighted series per panel, which is why these facet by site
rather than by month. Tried the other way round — a facet per month with all eight sites
coloured on top of the twelve months in grey — the panel carries twenty curves, the grey
stops reading as context and turns to haze. The pattern needs something to contrast
against.

Read down the column: PacWave South and North sit furthest right and are broad, Humboldt
just behind them, and San Luis Obispo is both furthest left and narrow — a small resource
that is at least consistent. WETS is the narrowest of all.

```{python}
for q, label in QOIS.items():
    ecdf = q == "J"  # too heavy-tailed for a density on a linear axis
    g = sns.displot(
        data=tidy, x=q, hue="Site", hue_order=site_labels, palette=label_colors,
        col="Site", col_order=site_labels, col_wrap=4,
        kind="ecdf" if ecdf else "kde", height=2.2, aspect=1.5, linewidth=2.2,
        facet_kws={"sharey": False}, legend=False, zorder=5,
    )
    for ax in g.axes.flat:  # the whole field, grey, behind every panel
        for other in site_labels:
            plot = sns.ecdfplot if ecdf else sns.kdeplot
            plot(data=tidy[tidy["Site"] == other], x=q,
                 color="0.75", linewidth=0.9, ax=ax, zorder=1)
    g.set_titles("{col_name}", size=9)
    if not ecdf:
        g.set(yticks=[])
    g.set_axis_labels(label, "Proportion below" if ecdf else "")
    g.figure.suptitle(f"{label}: each site against all eight", y=1.01, size=11)
    savefig(f"multisite_dist_vs_field_{q.lower()}")
```

## 10. Does a Longer Record Change the Answer?

Every contour so far carries the same caveat: one year is illustrative, not an extreme-value
estimate. A 100-year contour extrapolated from 2,920 sea states is asking a single year to
speak for a century, and the honest question is not whether that is wrong but *how* wrong —
which is answerable by simply doing it again with more years and looking at what moves.

`load_site` takes a year and caches per site-year, so a longer record is a concatenation
and nothing else. Change `CONTOUR_YEARS` to widen or narrow the window; the figures follow.
Each added year costs one download per site on first run and nothing after that.

```{python}
CONTOUR_YEARS = [2006, 2007, 2008, 2009, 2010]

# Nested windows, all ending at YEAR: 1 -> [2010], 5 -> [2006..2010]. Each is a
# superset of the one before, so any change between them comes from added data and
# not from swapping which years are in play.
YEAR_WINDOWS = {n: CONTOUR_YEARS[-n:] for n in range(1, len(CONTOUR_YEARS) + 1)}


def load_site_years(name, years=CONTOUR_YEARS):
    """One site's sea states across several years, concatenated in time order."""
    frames = [load_site(name, year=year) for year in years]
    return pd.concat(frames).sort_index()


# The five-year records are the largest cold-cache cost in the document: without
# them every contour below refits from scratch, which does not fit in a Kaggle
# session. Check the full site x year matrix before any of it starts.
require_cached(
    [CACHE_DIR / f"{name}_{year}.pkl"
     for name in SITES_TO_COMPARE for year in CONTOUR_YEARS],
    f"Multi-year records ({CONTOUR_YEARS[0]}-{CONTOUR_YEARS[-1]})",
)


# --- Contour cache -------------------------------------------------------------
# Fitting a contour is the expensive step in this document: the nonparametric and
# KDE methods take ~15 s each, and Sections 10-11 need hundreds of fits. Every fit
# is a pure function of (site, years, method, return_period), so it is cached to
# disk under .cache/contours/ and re-renders read it back in milliseconds. The
# result is small (two coordinate arrays), unlike the raw sea-state pickles.
# Set FORCE_CONTOURS = True to ignore the cache and recompute everything.
FORCE_CONTOURS = False
CONTOUR_CACHE_DIR = CACHE_DIR / "contours"
CONTOUR_CACHE_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)


def contour(name, years, method="PCA", return_period=100, xvar="Te", force=None):
    """100-year (default) contour for one site+record+method, memoised to disk.

    `xvar` picks the second variable: "Te" (energy period, the document default and
    the WEC-design convention) or "Tp" (spectral peak period, used where a J value
    has to be reconstructed from the contour — see the representative-extreme table).
    Returns a dict with 'x1' (Hm0) and 'x2' (the chosen period), or both None if the
    fit does not converge.
    """
    force = FORCE_CONTOURS if force is None else force
    # Te contours keep the original key (no suffix) so the existing cache stays valid.
    suffix = "" if xvar == "Te" else f"__{xvar}"
    key = f"{name}__{years[0]}-{years[-1]}__{method}__{return_period}yr{suffix}.pkl"
    path = CONTOUR_CACHE_DIR / key
    if path.exists() and not force:
        return pd.read_pickle(path)

    df = load_site_years(name, years=years)
    dt = (df.index[1] - df.index[0]).total_seconds()
    kwargs = {}
    if method.startswith("bivariate_KDE"):
        # Scale the bandwidth to each variable's own spread rather than one flat
        # number for both Hm0 (m) and the period (s).
        kwargs["bandwidth"] = [0.3 * df.Hm0.std(), 0.3 * df[xvar].std()]
    try:
        cop = contours.environmental_contours(
            df.Hm0, df[xvar], dt, return_period, method=method, **kwargs)
        result = {"x2": np.asarray(cop[f"{method}_x2"]),
                  "x1": np.asarray(cop[f"{method}_x1"])}
    except (np.linalg.LinAlgError, ValueError):
        result = {"x2": None, "x1": None}
    pd.to_pickle(result, path)
    return result


def contour_peak(name, years, method="PCA", return_period=100):
    """Peak Hm0 of a cached contour, or NaN if it did not converge."""
    hm0 = contour(name, years, method, return_period)["x1"]
    return np.nan if hm0 is None else float(np.nanmax(hm0))


record = pd.Series(
    {SITES[n]["label"]: len(load_site_years(n)) for n in site_order},
    name=f"sea states, {CONTOUR_YEARS[0]}–{CONTOUR_YEARS[-1]}",
)
print(record.to_string())
```

### The same contour, on a longer record

This is the Section 8 contour figure rebuilt on the full five-year record — same sites, same
colours, same method. It is tempting to read this as the corrected version of that one. The
rest of this section is about why that reading is wrong.

```{python}
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 6.5))
for name in site_order:
    cop = contour(name, CONTOUR_YEARS)  # cached PCA, 100-year
    if cop["x1"] is None:
        print(f"no contour for {SITES[name]['label']}")
        continue
    ax.plot(cop["x2"], cop["x1"], color=site_colors[name], linewidth=2.2,
            label=SITES[name]["label"])
ax.set(xlabel="Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]",
       ylabel="Significant Wave Height, $H_{m0}$ [m]")
ax.legend(title=f"100-year contour, {CONTOUR_YEARS[0]}–{CONTOUR_YEARS[-1]}",
          loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("multisite_contour_100yr_multiyear")
```

### The extreme against the length of the record

This is the section's real result. For each site, the peak of the 100-year contour —
the one number a designer takes away — recomputed on 1, 2, 3, 4 and 5 years, each window a
superset of the last.

If a longer record simply refined the estimate, these lines would settle: big moves early,
then a plateau. **They do not.** They wander, they cross, they reverse, and at the 4→5 year
step several are still moving by half a metre. PacWave South reads 11.2 m on 2010 alone,
9.2 m on three years, and 12.5 m on five — and the three-year value is the outlier, not the
one-year value.

The lesson is not "use more years". It is that within 1–5 years this estimate has not
converged at all, so **any single window is an arbitrary draw from a distribution of
answers**. A number quoted from one window without saying which window is not meaningful.
Watch PacWave South and North especially: a few kilometres apart, statistically
indistinguishable seas, and their lines are not even parallel.

```{python}
sweep = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        SITES[name]["label"]: {
            n: contour_peak(name, years) for n, years in YEAR_WINDOWS.items()
        }
        for name in site_order
    }
)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8.5, 5.5))
for name in site_order:
    label = SITES[name]["label"]
    ax.plot(sweep.index, sweep[label], marker="o", color=site_colors[name],
            linewidth=2, label=label)
ax.set(xlabel="Years of record (each window ends at 2010)",
       ylabel="Peak $H_{m0}$ of the 100-year contour [m]")
ax.set_xticks(list(YEAR_WINDOWS))
ax.legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("contour_peak_vs_record_length")
```

```{python}
table = sweep.T
table.columns = [f"{n} yr" for n in table.columns]
table["range [m]"] = (table.max(axis=1) - table.min(axis=1))
print(table.round(2).to_string())
```

The `range` column is the spread of answers a site gives depending only on how many years
you happened to use. For most of these sites it is 1–3 m of $H_{m0}$ on a ~10 m design
value, from a choice that has nothing to do with the ocean.

### The contour itself, at each record length

The same thing as curves rather than numbers: one panel per window length, every site drawn
in each. Watching a single colour across the five panels shows the shape moving, not just
its peak — and shows that the movement is not a steady convergence towards anything.

```{python}
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, len(YEAR_WINDOWS), figsize=(19, 4.2),
                         sharex=True, sharey=True)
for ax, (n, years) in zip(axes, YEAR_WINDOWS.items()):
    for name in site_order:
        cop = contour(name, years)  # cached PCA, 100-year
        if cop["x1"] is None:
            continue
        ax.plot(cop["x2"], cop["x1"], color=site_colors[name], linewidth=1.6,
                label=SITES[name]["label"] if n == len(YEAR_WINDOWS) else None)
    ax.set_title(f"{n} year{'s' if n > 1 else ''}  ({years[0]}–{years[-1]})", size=9)
    ax.set_xlabel("$T_e$ [s]")
axes[0].set_ylabel("$H_{m0}$ [m]")
axes[-1].legend(loc="upper left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 1), borderaxespad=0, fontsize=8)
fig.suptitle("100-year contour by length of record", size=11)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("contour_by_record_length")
```

## 11. Does the Method Change the Answer?

Section 10 showed that the length of the record moves the 100-year contour and never
settles. This section holds the record fixed — the full five years — and varies the
*method* instead. `contours.environmental_contours` offers ten (Section 4), and the
question is the same: how much of the final number is the ocean, and how much is the choice
of estimator?

One column per site, every method drawn in each, so the comparison happens within a site
rather than across the page. Reading down a column, the answer is stark: the estimators do
not agree, and they disagree by *more* than the record length moved things and far more than
the sites differ from one another for a fixed method.

```{python}
METHODS = {
    "PCA": "parametric",
    "gaussian": "parametric",
    "gumbel": "parametric",
    "clayton": "parametric",
    "rosenblatt": "parametric",
    "nonparametric_gaussian": "nonparametric",
    "nonparametric_clayton": "nonparametric",
    "nonparametric_gumbel": "nonparametric",
    "bivariate_KDE": "KDE",
    "bivariate_KDE_log": "KDE",
}
# One colour per family: the members of a family share a marginal fit and so give nearly
# identical peaks, which the plot should show as overlapping rather than eight rivals.
FAMILY_COLOR = {"parametric": "#1f77b4", "nonparametric": "#d62728", "KDE": "#2ca02c"}
```

Each of the 80 contours below (ten methods × eight sites, five-year record) goes through
the same disk cache as Section 10, so this section is slow only on its first render — after
that the plot and the summary table both read the fits back in milliseconds. The
nonparametric methods take ~15 s apiece to fit the first time.

```{python}
# bivariate_KDE_log fits the density in log space, which on this data throws a
# 100-year contour out to Te ~ 40 s at Hm0 ~ 0 -- a physically impossible period
# and an artifact of the method's tail, not a real sea state. Each panel is framed
# to the OTHER nine methods so that comparison stays readable; the KDE-log curve is
# still drawn and simply runs past the frame edge. Its peak is in the table below.
# Nothing is compressed -- the view is cropped to the physical Te range.
FRAME_METHODS = [m for m in METHODS if m != "bivariate_KDE_log"]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(len(site_order), 1, figsize=(7.5, 3.1 * len(site_order)),
                         sharex=False)
for ax, name in zip(axes, site_order):
    for method, family in METHODS.items():
        cop = contour(name, CONTOUR_YEARS, method=method)
        if cop["x1"] is None:
            print(f"no {method} for {SITES[name]['label']}")
            continue
        ax.plot(cop["x2"], cop["x1"], color=FAMILY_COLOR[family], linewidth=1.4, alpha=0.85)
    # Frame from the nine well-behaved methods; KDE-log runs off the right edge.
    # Contour arrays can carry NaNs, so reduce with nan-aware min/max.
    framed = [contour(name, CONTOUR_YEARS, method=m) for m in FRAME_METHODS]
    te = np.concatenate([c["x2"] for c in framed if c["x2"] is not None])
    hm0 = np.concatenate([c["x1"] for c in framed if c["x1"] is not None])
    ax.set_xlim(min(np.nanmin(te), 0), np.nanmax(te) * 1.05)
    ax.set_ylim(min(np.nanmin(hm0), 0), np.nanmax(hm0) * 1.10)
    ax.set_title(SITES[name]["label"], size=9, loc="left")
    ax.set(xlabel="Energy Period, $T_e$ [s]", ylabel="$H_{m0}$ [m]")
handles = [plt.Line2D([], [], color=c, linewidth=2, label=f)
           for f, c in FAMILY_COLOR.items()]
axes[0].legend(handles=handles, title="method family", loc="upper right", fontsize=8)
fig.suptitle("100-year contour by method, five-year record", size=11, y=1.005)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("contour_by_method_per_site")
```

Each panel is framed to the nine well-behaved methods. The tenth, `bivariate_KDE_log`
(green), runs off the right edge at every site — it fits the density in log space and
throws a contour tail out to $T_e \approx 40$ s at $H_{m0} \approx 0$, a period no real sea
state reaches. That is an artifact of the method's tail, not an estimate; its peak $H_{m0}$
is still reported in the table below, it is simply not allowed to set the frame.

The colouring is by family for a reason the numbers make clear: the five parametric methods
share one marginal Weibull fit for $H_{m0}$ and so return an almost identical peak,
differing only in the conditional $T_e$; the three nonparametric methods likewise agree with
each other; and `bivariate_KDE` sits close to `PCA`. Ten methods, but really only a few
distinct answers — and they still disagree by several metres.

```{python}
peak_by_method = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        SITES[name]["label"]: {
            method: contour_peak(name, CONTOUR_YEARS, method=method)
            for method in METHODS
        }
        for name in site_order
    }
).T
peak_by_method["method range [m]"] = (
    peak_by_method.max(axis=1) - peak_by_method.min(axis=1)
)
print(peak_by_method.round(1).to_string())
```

Set that `method range` column beside the `range` column from Section 10. For every site,
**the choice of method moves the 100-year $H_{m0}$ by more than five years of record length
does** — for Pioneer WEC, about 16 m against 3. The single most consequential decision in an
extreme-value analysis of this data is not how many years to buy or which site to trust; it
is which line in the MHKiT documentation to pass as `method`, and that decision is currently
made by copying whatever the last example used. That is the honest and slightly
uncomfortable conclusion of this whole exercise.


## 12. A Representative Extreme, One Number per Site

Sections 10 and 11 showed that the 100-year extreme moves with the record and moves even
more with the method — ten methods give a wide spread and no single one is "correct".
That is honest but not yet *actionable*: a project needs one design number per site,
comparable across sites.

The neutral way to collapse the spread is the **median of the per-method maxima**. For each
site, take all ten methods' 100-year contours; on each, read the maximum $H_{m0}$ and the
maximum energy flux $J$; the representative extreme is the median across the ten. The median
is deliberate — the maximum across methods would be cherry-picking the worst case, and
`bivariate_KDE_log`'s outlier is neutralised by taking a median rather than a max.

Two details make $J$ honest here. The contour carries only $H_{m0}$ and a period, not $J$,
so $J$ is reconstructed at each contour point by building a JONSWAP spectrum and integrating
it with `mhkit`'s `energy_flux` at the site's **real water depth** (a finite-depth
calculation, not the deep-water shortcut). And the period used is the **measured peak
period** $T_p$ carried through from the hindcast — not converted from $T_e$ by a formula —
so the contours in this section are fitted in $(H_{m0}, T_p)$ space rather than
$(H_{m0}, T_e)$.

```{python}
# Water depth per site, read once from the hindcast metadata and cached to disk.
# meta is a compound table; reading one row at the site's gid is cheap (unlike the
# full-table scan that nearest_gid deliberately avoids).
DEPTH_CACHE = CACHE_DIR / "site_depths.pkl"
if DEPTH_CACHE.exists() and not FORCE_CONTOURS:
    site_depths = pd.read_pickle(DEPTH_CACHE)
else:
    site_depths = {}
    for name in site_order:
        gid = load_site(name).attrs["gid"]
        with Resource(region_path(SITE_REGIONS[name], YEAR)) as handle:
            row = handle.h5["meta"][gid]
            fields = dict(zip(row.dtype.names, row))
            site_depths[name] = float(fields["depth" if "depth" in fields else "water_depth"])
    pd.to_pickle(site_depths, DEPTH_CACHE)
```

```{python}
GAMMA = 3.3
_JONSWAP_FREQ = np.linspace(0.001, 1.0, 300)  # Hz


def contour_max_J(hm0, tp, depth, stride=15):
    """Largest energy flux [kW/m] on a (Hm0, Tp) contour.

    J is not carried on the contour, so it is reconstructed at each contour point:
    a JONSWAP spectrum from that point's (Hs = Hm0, Tp), integrated by mhkit's
    energy_flux at the site's real water depth. Tp is the measured peak period, not
    a conversion of Te.
    """
    best = np.nan
    for i in range(0, len(hm0), stride):
        H, T = hm0[i], tp[i]
        if not (np.isfinite(H) and np.isfinite(T)) or H <= 0 or T <= 0:
            continue
        spectrum = resource.jonswap_spectrum(_JONSWAP_FREQ, float(T), float(H), gamma=GAMMA)
        flux = resource.energy_flux(spectrum, depth, deep=False)
        best = np.nanmax([best, float(np.asarray(flux).squeeze()) / 1000.0])
    return best


# Median-of-maxima across the ten methods, cached (the J reconstruction is the one
# non-trivial cost left after the contour cache). Recompute with FORCE_CONTOURS.
REPEXT_CACHE = CACHE_DIR / f"repext_{CONTOUR_YEARS[0]}-{CONTOUR_YEARS[-1]}.pkl"
if REPEXT_CACHE.exists() and not FORCE_CONTOURS:
    representative = pd.read_pickle(REPEXT_CACHE)
else:
    rows = {}
    for name in site_order:
        hm0_max, j_max = [], []
        for method in METHODS:
            cop = contour(name, CONTOUR_YEARS, method=method, xvar="Tp")
            if cop["x1"] is None:
                continue
            hm0, tp = np.asarray(cop["x1"]), np.asarray(cop["x2"])
            hm0_max.append(np.nanmax(hm0))
            j_max.append(contour_max_J(hm0, tp, site_depths[name]))
        rows[SITES[name]["label"]] = {
            "Hm0 [m]": np.median(hm0_max),
            "Hm0 min": np.min(hm0_max), "Hm0 max": np.max(hm0_max),
            "J [kW/m]": np.median(j_max),
            "J min": np.min(j_max), "J max": np.max(j_max),
            "depth [m]": site_depths[name],
        }
    representative = pd.DataFrame(rows).T
    pd.to_pickle(representative, REPEXT_CACHE)
```

### The actionable comparison

One bar per site: the representative 100-year $H_{m0}$ and $J$, sorted. This is what all the
preceding analysis reduces to — a single survival number per site that a designer can act
on, with sites directly comparable.

```{python}
order = representative.sort_values("Hm0 [m]").index  # ascending -> largest on top in barh
y = np.arange(len(order))
bar_colors = [label_colors[label] for label in order]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 5))
for ax, col, fmt, title in (
    (axes[0], "Hm0 [m]", "%.1f", "Representative 100-yr $H_{m0}$ [m]"),
    (axes[1], "J [kW/m]", "%.0f", "Representative 100-yr $J$ [kW/m]"),
):
    bars = ax.barh(y, representative.loc[order, col], color=bar_colors)
    ax.bar_label(bars, fmt=fmt, padding=3, fontsize=9, color="0.25")
    ax.set_yticks(y)
    ax.set_yticklabels(order if col == "Hm0 [m]" else [])
    ax.set_title(title, size=10)
    ax.margins(x=0.14)
fig.tight_layout()
savefig("representative_extreme_bars")
```

The full breakdown, including the min–max range each site spans across the ten methods —
the interactive table (HTML/notebook) is sortable and filterable; the PDF shows it static.
The `min`/`max` columns are the honest reminder that the single bar above is a median over
a wide method spread, not a precise figure.

```{python}
show_table(
    representative.round(1),
    caption="Representative 100-year extreme per site (median across 10 methods)",
)
```
